confirmed_primary.title: Transient backend error in has_reference() reads as "no reference" — PUT then overwrites (or deletes) a live reference.bin, silently corrupting every sibling delta in the deltaspace
confirmed_primary.scenario: Deltaspace releases/v1 holds reference.bin + N delta objects on an S3/MinIO backend. A new PUT to releases/v1/new.zip arrives while the backend momentarily throttles or times out the HEAD on reference.bin. `StorageBackend::has_reference` returns bool, and the S3 impl is `object_exists` = `head_object().send().await.is_ok()` (src/storage/s3.rs:967-975) — a 503/timeout/500 is indistinguishable from NotFound, so `has_existing_reference` is false. store_inner then calls set_reference_baseline → put_reference, an UNCONDITIONAL PutObject (src/storage/s3.rs:1316-1333) that overwrites reference.bin with the new object's bytes. All N pre-existing deltas were encoded against the old reference bytes, which are now gone: every subsequent GET of those objects reconstructs wrong bytes and fails the SHA-256 gate (ChecksumMismatch → 500), permanently — the data is unrecoverable. The same false-negative also fires in store_spooled_delta (store.rs:403→411-421, put_reference_from_file) and, in the ratio-lost branch of commit_delta_or_passthrough (store.rs:692-700), causes delete_reference on a reference that actually pre-existed — same blast radius. The prefix lock does not help: the lock is held, the check itself is wrong.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:187-209 — `let has_existing_reference = self.storage.has_reference(...).await;` then `else { ... self.set_reference_baseline(&ctx).await? }` with no error channel; src/storage/s3.rs:967-975 `object_exists` maps every SdkError (throttle, timeout, 5xx) to `false`; src/storage/s3.rs:1316-1333 put_reference is an unconditional overwrite. Trait signature `async fn has_reference(&self, ...) -> bool` (src/storage/traits.rs) cannot propagate the error.

confirmed_primary.title: Buffered GET pre-decode guard rejects legitimately stored delta objects: reference+delta > max_object_size makes an accepted object permanently unreadable
confirmed_primary.scenario: Default config: max_object_size=100MB, max_delta_ratio=0.75, spool threshold=max_object_size. PUT A = 80MB delta-eligible file to prefix p/ (becomes the 80MB reference). PUT B = 90MB file ~50% different from A → xdelta3 delta ≈ 45MB, ratio 0.5 < 0.75 → committed as delta, PUT returns 200. GET B: file_size (90MB) ≤ spool threshold so it takes the buffered path; `combined_size = reference.len() + delta.len()` = 80+45 = 125MB > 100MB → EngineError::TooLarge → client gets EntityTooLarge on every GET of an object the proxy accepted and stored correctly. Deterministic and permanent under unchanged config (recoverable only by raising max_object_size or lowering DGP_SPOOL_THRESHOLD_BYTES to force the spooled path, which has no such check). The guard's premise is wrong: ref+delta is NOT a lower bound for the reconstruction size — the reconstruction is exactly metadata.file_size, which is already ≤ max_object_size by the store-time gate. Replication/lifecycle copies of such objects (engine.retrieve) fail the same way.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/deltaglider/engine/retrieve.rs:627-633 — `let combined_size = reference.len() as u64 + delta.len() as u64; if combined_size > self.max_object_size { return Err(EngineError::TooLarge ...) }` with comment claiming combined size is 'a lower bound for the reconstructed object' (false). Store side only gates on ratio (store.rs:663-664) and individual sizes, never on ref_size+delta_size, so objects violating the read-side guard are freely committed. Defaults: src/config/mod.rs:1115-1121 (ratio 0.75, max 100MB).

confirmed_primary.title: Spool budget under-reservation: the reference spool is reserved at the OBJECT's file_size, but the reference written into it can be up to max_object_size — bypasses the ENOSPC byte-budget the spool exists to enforce
confirmed_primary.scenario: Config with DGP_SPOOL_THRESHOLD_BYTES=8MB (the setting that activates the spooled paths at all), max_object_size=100MB. A deltaspace's reference is a 100MB baseline; sibling objects are 10MB deltas (file_size 10MB > 8MB threshold → spooled GET path). reconstruct_delta_to_spool reserves acquire_pair(10MB, 10MB) = 20MiB of budget, then get_reference_to_file writes the full 100MB reference into ref_spool — 80MB of unaccounted disk per request. Under concurrency (the exact case the budget guards): 100 parallel such GETs reserve ~2GiB of a 16GiB budget but physically write ~11GB; scale concurrency and the spool dir/tmpfs hits ENOSPC, failing unrelated in-flight codec ops — the precise failure mode the SpoolDir byte-budget was built to prevent (spool.rs module doc). store_spooled_delta has the same bug (acquire_pair(size, size) at store.rs:441, then materialises a possibly-larger reference into the first spool).
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/deltaglider/engine/retrieve.rs:249-257 — `spool_acquire_pair(metadata.file_size, metadata.file_size)` followed by `get_reference_to_file(...)` whose payload is the REFERENCE's size (up to max_object_size), not the object's; src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:441-444 identical shape. Reference size is knowable up front via get_reference_metadata but never used for the reservation. Budget semantics: src/deltaglider/spool.rs:10-14, 123-133 (permits sized purely by the requested byte count; nothing enforces actual file size).

confirmed_primary.title: Buffered-path watchdog ignores spurious condvar wakeups — can SIGKILL a healthy xdelta3 mid-encode/decode before the timeout has elapsed
confirmed_primary.scenario: pipe_stdin_stdout_stderr's watchdog thread performs a single `condvar.wait_timeout(guard, timeout)` and discards the WaitTimeoutResult; it then treats `!flag` as "timeout expired". std::sync::Condvar::wait_timeout is documented to be subject to spurious wakeups. If one occurs at, say, t=2s into a legitimate 8s 100MB encode, the flag is still false (I/O in progress), so the watchdog SIGKILLs the healthy child: the PUT/GET fails with "xdelta3 CLI failed" (empty stderr) even though nothing was wrong — a sporadic, unreproducible 5xx. The streaming watchdog (pipe_streaming:299-327) loops and re-checks actual progress, so it is immune; only the buffered path has the single-shot wait.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/deltaglider/codec.rs:173-204 — `let _result = condvar.wait_timeout(guard, timeout).unwrap(); if !flag.load(...) { libc::kill(child_id, SIGKILL) }`: no loop, and `_result.1.timed_out()` is never consulted, so any wake (spurious or otherwise) before `done` is set is treated as a timeout.

confirmed_primary.title: store_spooled_delta never verifies the spooled byte count against the declared size — a short/long spool is stored with mismatched file_size, producing Content-Length ≠ body on later GETs
confirmed_primary.scenario: transfer.rs:1048-1082 streams a source object to a spool and calls store_spooled_delta with `source_size` taken from the source's metadata snapshot, not from bytes actually written. If the streamed bytes differ from source_size (source overwritten between the metadata read and the stream, or a stale/corrupt xattr file_size on a filesystem-backend source), the hash step (store.rs:353-374) hashes whatever is in the file but stamps metadata.file_size = declared size — with no `observed != total_size` check, unlike every sibling ingest path (store.rs:962-967, 1043-1048 both reject on mismatch). The destination object then has sha256-of-actual-bytes + wrong file_size: GETs pass the SHA gate but advertise a Content-Length that disagrees with the body (client-side truncation/hang), and the spooled-GET output cap (retrieve.rs:279) truncates a longer-than-declared reconstruction into a hard decode error.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:352-374 — hashing loop counts nothing and performs no comparison against `size`; contrast store.rs:962-967 and store.rs:1043-1048 where the equivalent relay paths enforce `observed != total_size → error`. Caller passing a metadata-derived size over freshly streamed bytes: src/transfer.rs:1048-1077.

confirmed_primary.title: resolve_existing treats head_bucket ERRORS as 'bucket absent' — a transient default-backend failure silently reroutes reads/writes to a same-named bucket on another backend
confirmed_primary.scenario: Bucket 'backups' exists on BOTH the default backend (hetzner) and a secondary backend (aws-dr) — a common name collision across accounts. The default backend's HEAD returns a transient error (503/connect reset). resolve_existing does `default.head_bucket(virtual_bucket).await.unwrap_or(false)` → error becomes false → the name-sorted scan finds 'backups' on aws-dr → the client's PUT is written into aws-dr's unrelated 'backups' bucket (its deltaspace/reference.bin too). When the default backend recovers seconds later, GET routes back to hetzner → the client's own just-written object 404s, and the secondary bucket is silently polluted with foreign objects. Same hole in resolve_existing_named (lines 410, 424), so multipart uploads can be stamped to the wrong backend for the whole upload lifetime.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/routing.rs:232 `if default.head_bucket(virtual_bucket).await.unwrap_or(false)` and :243 `if backend.head_bucket(virtual_bucket).await.unwrap_or(false)` — Err is collapsed to false, so 'backend temporarily unreachable' is indistinguishable from 'bucket not there', and the fall-through scan (236-246) actively routes to any other backend holding the same name. resolve_existing_named repeats this at :410/:424. Every object op goes through route_existing! (:252-257). Unlike the listing path (which got cooldown+timeout+last-known handling in 76b07ae), the resolve path has no error/health awareness at all.

confirmed_primary.title: Freshness invalidation lost to an in-flight probe: create/delete_bucket read-after-write guarantee is broken under concurrent listings
confirmed_primary.scenario: T0: a listing (e.g. the GUI 3s poller) enters gather_listings, decides to probe, and the upstream ListBuckets executes. T0+50ms: another request completes create_bucket('b2') → invalidate_listing_freshness() sets every last_known timestamp to None. T0+80ms: the first request's probe (whose upstream LIST ran BEFORE the create) reaches the health-update block and inserts (Some(now), stale_roster) — resurrecting a FRESH marker on a roster that lacks 'b2'. Every ListBuckets for the next list_fresh (5s default) serves the stale roster: the client that just got 200 from CreateBucket does not see its bucket in the very next listing. Same for delete (a deleted bucket keeps being listed). The regression test create_bucket_invalidates_fresh_window (line 1593) only covers the serial case.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/routing.rs:337 `h.last_known.insert(name.clone(), (Some(now), list.clone()))` runs unconditionally after the un-locked probe await (313-328), with `now` captured at :266 before the probe; invalidate_listing_freshness (:359-364) only mutates entries present at that instant and carries no generation/epoch, so a post-invalidate insert from an older probe wins. create_bucket/delete_bucket invalidate at :443/:451 but nothing prevents the resurrect.

confirmed_primary.title: Cooldown re-arm before probe + request cancellation pins a recovered backend in 'failure cooldown' with a stale error
confirmed_primary.scenario: Backend goes down → cooldown armed. Backend recovers, but is slow to answer its first ListBuckets (cold reconnect). At each cooldown expiry, exactly one request re-arms the cooldown (line 303-305, holding the stale error) and becomes the sole prober; every OTHER concurrent request is short-circuited with the cooldown error for the next full period. If that prober's future is cancelled before the health-update block runs (hyper drops the handler future when the client disconnects — e.g. the GUI's 3s poll abort racing a multi-second first probe), the success/failure update at 332-346 never executes: the backend stays flagged 'in failure cooldown: <old error>' for another full list_cooldown (30s) even though it is healthy, and its buckets keep rendering as unavailable placeholders. Repeated aborts extend this indefinitely; there is no other path that clears the cooldown.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/routing.rs:301-306 re-arms `h.cooldown.insert(name, (now + self.list_cooldown, last_err))` BEFORE the probe; the probe await (313-328) and the clearing `h.cooldown.remove(name)` (:338) run in the caller's request future with no spawn/detach, so cancellation between re-arm and update leaves the pessimistic state committed. All sibling requests in the window took the `deadline > now` branch (:292-299) and returned the cooldown error, so nobody else probes until the next expiry — where the same race can repeat.

confirmed_primary.title: Throttle-aborted HEAD enrichment poisons the metadata cache with stub delta metadata, making subsequent GETs of delta objects fail persistently with ChecksumMismatch
confirmed_primary.scenario: Backend answers 503 SlowDown (the exact condition the new breaker handles, e.g. the Hetzner 2026-07-09 RCA). A client or /_/stats issues ListObjectsV2 with metadata=true over a folder of delta-stored objects. In enrich_list_metadata, one batch comes back >=50% throttled (throttled*2 >= batch.len(), s3.rs:1982) -> the sweep aborts and every remaining delta entry is returned with its FALLBACK lite metadata: StorageInfo::delta_stub (ref_path/ref_sha256/delta_cmd all empty), file_size = delta size, file_sha256 = "" (fallback_metadata_from_listing, s3.rs:1104-1131 + types.rs:376-396). The engine then caches EVERY returned entry indiscriminately (engine/mod.rs:1398-1400). A subsequent S3 GetObject on any of those objects hits the cache (retrieve.rs:48), takes the Delta branch, reconstructs correctly, but the mandatory checksum gate compares the 64-hex actual sha against the cached "" -> EngineError::ChecksumMismatch -> the GET fails (retrieve.rs:674-694), and each failed GET also evicts the deltaspace reference cache (retrieve.rs:681). The stale-cache retry only fires on NotFound (retrieve.rs:95), never on ChecksumMismatch. Worse, the cache-hit path RE-INSERTS the poisoned entry on every GET (retrieve.rs:60), resetting the moka 10-min TTL — a client polling GET keeps the object unreadable indefinitely until a HEAD/PUT/DELETE on that key or a process restart. One throttled listing can poison hundreds of objects at once.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/s3.rs:1976-2005 — after the breaker `break`, the zip at 1995-2004 silently substitutes `fallback_meta` (lite delta_stub with empty file_sha256) for every un-HEAD'd key, indistinguishable from real enrichment. src/deltaglider/engine/mod.rs:1392-1400 — `enrich_list_metadata` results are unconditionally inserted into `metadata_cache` (`self.metadata_cache.insert(bucket, key, meta.clone())`). src/deltaglider/engine/retrieve.rs:48-61 — GET consults the cache first and re-inserts the cached value (TTL renewal); :674-694 — `actual_sha256 != metadata.file_sha256` ("") -> ChecksumMismatch error; :95 — only `EngineError::NotFound` triggers cache invalidation+retry. metadata_cache.rs has no stub/empty-sha guard on insert. Pre-f77b740 this poisoning needed an individual HEAD failure; the new breaker (f77b740) makes it deterministic and mass-scale under throttle — the very scenario it was built for.

confirmed_primary.title: classify_s3_error throttle truth-table hole: HTTP 429 (Backblaze B2 / Cloudflare R2 rate limiting) is not mapped to StorageError::Throttled
confirmed_primary.scenario: A supported non-AWS backend that throttles with HTTP 429 (Backblaze B2 — an explicitly supported replication target — and Cloudflare R2 both use 429 Too Many Requests, with bodies like 'TooManyRequests', not 'SlowDown'). After the SDK's adaptive retries exhaust, the 429 reaches classify_s3_error: it matches neither `s == 503` nor `debug_str.contains("SlowDown")` (s3.rs:429), so it falls to `StorageError::S3(...)` (s3.rs:435) -> the API layer's catch-all -> HTTP 500 InternalError to the client. This recreates exactly the E-P1-1 cascade the code's own comment describes (s3.rs:418-428): AWS SDK clients treat 500 as permanent and don't back off, producing retry storms against an already-throttling backend. Additionally, `bounded_head_calls` counts only `StorageError::Throttled` (s3.rs:1080), so against a 429-throttling backend the new HEAD circuit-breaker (s3.rs:1982) NEVER trips and the enrichment sweep grinds through the full key list, defeating the protection.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/s3.rs:414-434 — the Throttled mapping covers only `s == 503` and the literal substring "SlowDown"; no `s == 429`, no 'TooManyRequests'/'RequestLimitExceeded' code handling. s3.rs:1080 — the breaker counts exclusively `Err(StorageError::Throttled(_))`. The comment at s3.rs:418-428 states the intent is to catch 'the AWS-spec transient throttle signal' so it doesn't become a 500 — 429 is the equally standard throttle status on several supported S3-compatible providers.

confirmed_primary.title: list_fetch_boundary strips one '.delta' (strip_suffix) while classify_listed_objects strips ALL trailing '.delta' (trim_end_matches) — divergent user-key mapping for keys ending '.delta.delta' can end a listing with a false is_truncated=false, silently omitting objects
confirmed_primary.scenario: Keys ending '.delta.delta' can only enter via out-of-band writes to the backend bucket (ingest rejects user keys ending '.delta', types.rs:133), but the proxy explicitly supports foreign/unmanaged objects. For raw key 'b.delta.delta': the boundary counts user key 'b.delta' (one strip, s3.rs:2243) while classification serves user key 'b' (trim_end_matches strips both, s3.rs:1017) — so pairs like 'b.delta'+'b.delta.delta' count as TWO distinct candidates in the boundary but dedup to ONE served entry. Construct: >2000 raw keys where ~1000 are such external pairs; on a delimiter-less ListObjectsV2 with max_keys=1000, after upstream page 2 the boundary sees >=1001 'distinct' candidates and the last raw key exceeds the horizon -> early exit; classification then dedups the fetched set down to exactly <=1000 entries -> interleave_and_paginate returns is_truncated=false with next_token=None (engine/mod.rs:115-123) while unfetched keys remain upstream -> those objects silently vanish from the listing (clients believe the list is complete). The same premise break also invalidates the horizon proof: for anchor U='x.a', horizon='x.delta' does not cover raw 'x.delta.delta' (> horizon) whose served user key 'x' sorts before U.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/s3.rs:2243-2247 — `key.strip_suffix(".delta")` strips exactly once; src/storage/s3.rs:1015-1020 — `filename.trim_end_matches(".delta")` strips repeatedly (`"b.delta.delta".trim_end_matches(".delta") == "b"`). The doc-comment's completeness argument (s3.rs:2217-2221, 'A raw key maps to a user key by at most stripping `.delta`') is falsified by the classifier the result is fed into (s3.rs:2146-2148). Trigger requires externally-written *.delta.delta keys aligned with upstream page boundaries — contrived, hence LOW — but the two mappers disagreeing is a latent soundness hole in a function whose correctness rests on them agreeing.

confirmed_primary.title: put_reference_metadata builds x-amz-copy-source without URL-encoding — legal key characters ('%', '?', '+') in a deltaspace prefix break (or misdirect) the legacy-reference migration CopyObject
confirmed_primary.scenario: Key validation accepts '%', '?', '+', '#' in prefixes (types.rs:168-192 only rejects NUL, backslash, '..'). A legacy deltaspace under prefix 'sale 50% off/' (first object stored pre-migration): on the next PUT into that deltaspace, migrate-legacy-reference runs put_delta + delete_passthrough successfully, then put_reference_metadata sends CopyObject with copy_source = 'bucket/sale 50% off/reference.bin' verbatim (s3.rs:1358). Per the S3 spec (and the aws-sdk-rust copy_source contract) the value must be URL-encoded by the caller and is URL-DECODED server-side: '%' followed by ' o' is an invalid percent-escape -> AWS/MinIO reject with 400 -> the PUT fails, and it fails identically on every retry (the passthrough was already deleted at s3.rs:1357/store.rs:1357, so the deltaspace is stuck mid-migration with ref_meta.original_name never flipped). With '?' in the prefix, the substring after '?' is parsed as a versionId subresource — on a lenient provider the DATA of a different object could be copied over reference.bin (MetadataDirective::Replace copies source content), corrupting every delta in the deltaspace.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/storage/s3.rs:1358 — `let copy_source = format!("{}/{}", bucket, key);` with no percent-encoding; aws-sdk-s3's CopyObject `copy_source` is passed into the x-amz-copy-source header verbatim and its documentation requires the caller to URL-encode it. Caller: src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:1360-1363 (legacy reference migration, invoked from the PUT pipeline), after the passthrough deletion at store.rs:1357 — so the failure leaves the migration permanently half-done and every subsequent PUT into that deltaspace erroring.

confirmed_primary.title: Poison-object skip is permanent: nothing can ever clear a poisoned object, and the new throttle-abort mass-produces poisoned objects during a sustained throttling incident
confirmed_primary.scenario: Destination backend throttles (real 503 SlowDown) for ~5 backed-off runs (backoff floor is 60s, so a <10-minute incident suffices at min cadence). Each run: the throttle gate (worker.rs:584) aborts with the cursor kept, so every subsequent run re-lists the SAME page; every object on that page records one consecutive failure per run (worker.rs:946-951). After object_skip_after_failures=5 (default, config_sections.rs:434-438) runs, the entire page's objects hit the threshold and the poison guard (worker.rs:859-871) skips them. Skipped objects never reach copy_one_object, so replication_clear_object_failure — the ONLY code that ever deletes a ledger row (state_store.rs:645-656; grep shows no other caller, no admin reset endpoint, no time decay in replication_object_skipped state_store.rs:660-680) — can never run. When the backend recovers, those objects are silently skipped in every future run forever, the run reports 'succeeded', and the destination permanently diverges from source. Same permanent poisoning for any dest outage spanning 5 runs (the prod B2-over-quota shape).
confirmed_primary.evidence: worker.rs:858-871 skips before any copy attempt ('Reset on success below' — but success is unreachable once skipped). state_store.rs:660-680 checks only consecutive_failures >= threshold with no last_failed_at decay. state_store.rs:645-656 (clear) is called solely from worker.rs:937 inside the Ok(copy) arm. Throttle abort keeps the cursor (break at worker.rs:604 before pager.advance/persist at worker.rs:653-668; settle_run worker.rs:1146 keeps cursor on hit_fatal_error), so the identical page's objects re-fail every backed-off run until poisoned.

confirmed_primary.title: Scheduler and admin run-now use disjoint lease stores when a coordination (S3) lease is configured — two concurrent runs of the same rule, breaking the run-now 409 contract and the single-writer-per-deltaspace invariant
confirmed_primary.scenario: config_sync_bucket is set, so the scheduler serializes runs via the S3-CAS lease object (scheduler.rs:103-112, S3Lease::try_acquire s3_lease.rs:207 — never touches SQLite). Operator clicks Run Now while a scheduled run is mid-flight: the handler checks only the node-local SQLite lease table (replication.rs:108-121, db.replication_try_acquire_lease), which nobody holds → acquire succeeds instead of the intended 409 ('rule is already running'), and run_rule is spawned with coordination_lease=None (replication.rs:192). Two runs of the same rule now execute concurrently: each persists its own continuation_token under the same rule_name key (worker.rs:660), so a third run/crash-resume starts from whichever mid-run token was written last and silently skips the head of the keyspace for that pass; whichever run finishes first clears the cursor and (if replicate_deletes) runs the delete pass while the other is still copying. Cross-node (scheduler leader on node B, run-now on node A), both engines write the same destination deltaspace concurrently — the documented reference.bin corruption hazard (single-writer per deltaspace is only enforced by the in-process prefix lock).
confirmed_primary.evidence: replication.rs:102-126 acquires ONLY the SQLite lease and its comment claims 'a double run-now still 409s' — true only vs another run-now; scheduler.rs:35-36 shows the SQLite path (LocalLease, lease.rs:91-102) is used by the scheduler only when NO coordination lease is injected. With S3Lease injected the two arbiters are disjoint objects, so the mutual exclusion that holds in default single-instance mode silently disappears exactly when coordination is enabled.

confirmed_primary.title: Kill dropping the page's collect future can leave a durably-successful copy's consecutive-failure count uncleared, later poisoning an object whose failures were not consecutive
confirmed_primary.scenario: Object X has consecutive_failures=4 (threshold 5). During a run its copy completes durably and the event is pushed (worker.rs:918-934, pre-await — good), but the operator kills the run and the biased select (worker.rs:524-531) drops the copy_page future while copy_one_object is parked on db.lock().await at worker.rs:936 — replication_clear_object_failure never executes. The ledger still says 4 despite the success. A single later transient failure bumps it to 5 → the object is permanently poison-skipped (see finding 1) even though it never actually failed 5 consecutive runs. The copied object's totals delta is also lost (run history undercounts objects_copied/bytes for the cancelled run — cosmetic).
confirmed_primary.evidence: worker.rs:917 comment guards the EVENT against the kill-drop ('push the event BEFORE any further await') but the failure-ledger clear at worker.rs:935-938 sits after that comment's protected point, behind another await on the shared ConfigDb mutex; the tokio::select! at worker.rs:524 ('biased; killed_now = poll_run_killed…') drops all in-flight copy futures at their current await point when the kill arm wins.

confirmed_primary.title: Throttle-abort gate is dead code for rules with batch_size <= 2, and mixed throttle/other-error pages (e.g. 2 throttled + N real errors, 0 copies) never trigger the backoff
confirmed_primary.scenario: A rule configured with batch_size: 2 (cap = rule.batch_size.clamp(1, 10_000), worker.rs:251) lists at most 2 objects per page, so page_throttled >= 3 is unsatisfiable — a fully-throttling backend (the exact prod incident the gate was added for) is ground through page-by-page with per-object retry loops and NO backend_throttled backoff; the run ends 'failed' with next_due at normal cadence and clear_cursor=true, then the next run grinds the whole keyspace again from the start. Similarly a page with exactly 2 SlowDown rejections plus other-shaped errors and 0 copies continues to the next page instead of backing off. Not data corruption — sustained pointless load against a backend that is shedding load, i.e. the behavior the gate exists to prevent.
confirmed_primary.evidence: worker.rs:584 requires page_throttled >= 3 with page_throttled counted per-object per-page only (worker.rs:560-562); page size is bounded by cap from rule.batch_size at worker.rs:251/338, so small-batch rules can never reach 3. No fallback (e.g. page_throttled == page_size or a cross-page throttle accumulator) exists.

confirmed_primary.title: Unresolved HEADs (and the head_burst full-batch abort) remove keys from only ONE side of the diff, converting in-sync pairs into false OrphanOnDest / MissingOnDest counts, samples, and delete-flavored remediation
confirmed_primary.scenario: Transforming regime (e.g. S3 source or S3/encrypting dest). Source side: phase-3 prune (parity.rs:1220-1225) guarantees every key in src_needs_logical has a matching dest key. If the HEAD for key K gets a transient 503, HeadOutcome::Unresolved makes resolve_logical do `source.remove(&dk)` (parity.rs:748-751) while dest still holds K — diff_parity then classifies K as OrphanOnDest: orphan_on_dest increments, an orphan sample is emitted, and analyze_orphan (remediation.rs:254-296) annotates it as either 'mirror-delete removes the orphaned copy' (owned + replicate_deletes) or ForeignOrphan with fix DeleteFromDest{foreign:true} / 'delete it manually if it shouldn't be there' — for an object that is actually identical on both sides. Amplified by the batch abort: on a throttled dest (the exact Hetzner 503 SlowDown case the abort was added for), one first batch of >=3 all-Unresolved HEADs (parity.rs:896-911) mass-marks EVERY remaining key Unresolved; on a non-authoritative dest dst_needs_logical contains ALL dest keys (parity.rs:1230), so resolve_logical_dest removes nearly the whole dest map (parity.rs:825-828) and the outcome reports up to ~all source keys as MissingOnDest with remediation 'rerun_helps: Yes / RunNow' — a completely in-sync mirror is reported as almost entirely missing, and an operator following the guidance triggers a mass re-copy sweep (the prod re-copy incident class). truncated=true keeps in_sync=false honest, but the operator-facing counts/samples/remediation are fabricated findings, not 'partial coverage'.
confirmed_primary.evidence: parity.rs:748-751 (`HeadOutcome::Unresolved => { source.remove(&dk); unresolved += 1; }`) removes only the source entry; the paired dest entry inserted at parity.rs:1207 survives, and diff_parity (parity.rs:449-463) counts any dest-only key as OrphanOnDest. Phase-3 retain at parity.rs:1220-1225 (`dest.contains_key(&dk)`) proves every source key subject to this removal HAS a dest counterpart, so the false orphan is deterministic, not incidental. Mirror-image on the dest side: parity.rs:825-828 removes only the dest entry while the source entry remains → MissingOnDest (diff_parity parity.rs:434-447), and analyze_missing (remediation.rs:151-162) stamps it RerunVerdict::Yes/RunNow. The abort path parity.rs:896-911 extends `out` with EVERY remaining key as Unresolved, feeding the same one-sided removal for the whole resolve set. The code comments at parity.rs:747 and 822-824 say the intent is 'drop from the compare', but dropping the key from one map does not drop the PAIR from the compare — the fix is to remove the key from BOTH maps (and dest_to_source) so an unresolved pair contributes only to the `unresolved`/truncated partial-audit accounting.

confirmed_primary.title: Batch DeleteObjects bypasses per-key IAM authorization (Deny carve-outs / prefix scoping ignored)
confirmed_primary.scenario: An IAM user is granted `Allow delete` on `bucket/*` together with an explicit `Deny delete` on `bucket/protected/*` (a common carve-out). The user sends `POST /bucket?delete` (S3 DeleteObjects / batch delete) with a body listing `protected/secret.txt`. The axum authorization_middleware classifies this as `S3Action::Delete` on the BUCKET-LEVEL resource with an empty key (parse_bucket_key returns key=""), so it evaluates `user.can(Delete, "bucket", "")`. The Allow `bucket/*` matches the bucket-level ARN (via the empty-key trailing-slash alt path in evaluate_iam, line 280), while the Deny `bucket/protected/*` does NOT match `arn:aws:s3:::bucket` or `arn:aws:s3:::bucket/`, so the bucket-level check returns Allow and the request is admitted. The `delete_objects` handler (line 699) then iterates the body keys and calls `engine.delete(&input.bucket, &key)` unconditionally — it never fetches the `AuthenticatedUser` and never calls `user.can(S3Action::Delete, bucket, key)` per key. `protected/secret.txt` is deleted despite the explicit Deny. The same handler also lets a user whose only route to bucket-level delete is a whole-bucket Allow delete every key including those a Deny should protect.
confirmed_primary.evidence: delete_objects (src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:699-766) collects keys from `input.delete.objects` (request body) and calls `engine.delete` with no IAM check; it does not even read `AuthenticatedUser` from extensions. Contrast recursive_delete_prefix_s3s (src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:1483-1487) which correctly does `if !user.can(S3Action::Delete, bucket, obj_key) { denied += 1; continue; }` per key. The axum authorization_middleware only sees the request PATH (`/bucket`), classifying batch delete as bucket-level Delete with key="" (src/iam/middleware.rs:51-57, 92-98, 100, 228) — it cannot see the body keys, so per-key deny/prefix scoping must be enforced in the handler, and delete_objects omits it. check_client_write_allowed (src/api/handlers/object_helpers.rs:60-74) is only the replication-target/CAS marker gate, not IAM.

confirmed_primary.title: Periodic orphan relay sweep deletes part files of active uploads (promotion race + partial-promotion permanent orphan)
confirmed_primary.scenario: The periodic sweeper (src/main.rs:553, every 300s) calls sweep_orphan_relay_artifacts. Sequence: (1) sweep snapshots active relay dirs under uploads.read() — only uploads whose relay_strategy is ALREADY Relayed (multipart.rs:978-982) — and releases the lock; (2) a concurrent UploadPart crosses the relay threshold and promote_upload_to_relay (multipart.rs:997-1011) creates /tmp/deltaglider-mpu-relay/<upload_id>/ and writes all buffered parts to it (same for the first part of an always-relay upload created after the snapshot); (3) cleanup_orphan_relay_entries_at (multipart.rs:1106-1117) enumerates the relay root, the new dir is not in the stale snapshot, remove_dir_all deletes the active upload's part files. Later parts recreate the dir (write_part_file's create_dir_all), so the upload looks healthy until CompleteMultipartUpload: the buffered path fails in load_bytes (fs::read on a deleted file → InternalError 500, multipart.rs:54-56) and the relayed-passthrough path fails inside engine.store_passthrough_relayed_parts; rollback returns the upload to Open but the deleted parts can never validate again — the client must re-upload every part (for a large relayed upload, tens of GB). A second, PERSISTENT trigger: if promote_upload_to_relay errors partway (transient disk-full on one write_part_file), already-written parts are PartPayload::RelayedFile but upload.relay_strategy stays InMemory (only set at line 1009 after the loop), so the upload's relay dir is orphan-eligible on EVERY subsequent 5-minute sweep for the whole life of the upload — deterministic part loss, not a narrow window.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/multipart.rs:974-984: active set is built from `filter_map(|u| match &u.relay_strategy { RelayStrategy::Relayed { relay_dir } => Some(...), RelayStrategy::InMemory { .. } => None })` under a read lock that is released before deletion; src/multipart.rs:1109-1113 deletes any dir not in that snapshot. Promotion (multipart.rs:999 `fs::create_dir_all(&relay_dir)`) happens under the uploads WRITE lock, which does not serialize with the sweep's deletion phase (lock already released). Partial-failure case: multipart.rs:1002-1008 flips per-part payloads to RelayedFile inside the loop but `upload.relay_strategy = RelayStrategy::Relayed` only executes at line 1009 after every write succeeds, so an early `?` return leaves on-disk parts invisible to the sweep's active-set filter. Periodic invocation: src/main.rs:553 `let orphan_report = mp.sweep_orphan_relay_artifacts();` inside spawn_periodic — the function's own doc comment (multipart.rs:971-972) says 'Startup hardening', where the race would be benign (store is empty at src/main.rs:525), but it also runs every DGP_MULTIPART_SWEEP_INTERVAL_SECS.

confirmed_primary.title: Relay part files in fixed world-known temp path are trusted without content re-verification at Complete — local-attacker object substitution under valid ETag
confirmed_primary.scenario: Preconditions: proxy runs on a shared (non-containerized) host with other local users. Attacker pre-creates /tmp/deltaglider-mpu-relay (fixed name, multipart.rs:42; under std::env::temp_dir(), multipart.rs:1014-1016) before the proxy does — create_dir_all on an existing attacker-owned dir succeeds with no ownership/permission check. The attacker now owns the parent directory, so despite part files being 0600 NamedTempFiles, they can unlink and replace any part file (or swap the whole <upload_id> subdir) between UploadPart and CompleteMultipartUpload. At Complete, part content is never re-hashed: validate_parts compares the client-supplied ETag only against the in-memory md5_hex captured at upload time (multipart.rs:662-667), then the buffered path fs::read's the file verbatim (load_bytes, multipart.rs:54-56) and the relayed-passthrough path streams the file paths directly into engine storage (ordered_relay_part_paths, multipart.rs:1045-1063). Result: attacker-controlled bytes are stored as the client's object under the original, valid multipart ETag — silent data corruption/integrity bypass. Not exploitable in the documented Docker deployment (isolated /tmp), hence LOW.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/multipart.rs:42 `const RELAY_ROOT_DIR: &str = "deltaglider-mpu-relay";` + 1014-1016 `std::env::temp_dir().join(RELAY_ROOT_DIR)` — fixed predictable path, created via fs::create_dir_all (multipart.rs:999, 1030) which succeeds on a pre-existing attacker-owned directory. No re-hash on read: multipart.rs:54 `Self::RelayedFile(path) => fs::read(path)` with no MD5 check against the PartData.md5_raw recorded at write time (multipart.rs:437-444); validate_parts (multipart.rs:656-681) validates only the in-memory metadata, and the relayed passthrough complete (multipart.rs:558-561) hands raw paths to the engine with hydrate_part_data=false, so no byte of the substituted file is ever compared to the upload-time digest.

confirmed_primary.title: Config::load() silently falls back to full defaults when the config file fails to parse (incl. unset ${env:NAME}), then admin persist overwrites the operator's file with defaults
confirmed_primary.scenario: Operator runs the proxy with ./deltaglider_proxy.yaml found on the default search path (no --config, no DGP_CONFIG). The file contains `${env:HETZNER_SECRET}` and the env var is unset (or the file has any YAML/shape error). `from_file` returns Err(MissingEnvVar), the search-path loop swallows it with `if let Ok(...)` — no message of any kind — and `found.unwrap_or_default()` boots the proxy with Config::default() + env overrides: default filesystem backend ./data, NO named backends, NO bucket policies/public_prefixes, NO admission deny blocks, NO replication/lifecycle rules. If DGP_ACCESS_KEY_ID/SECRET are set in env (common in containers) the server serves S3 traffic against the wrong storage: existing objects appear deleted, new writes land in ./data, IP-deny admission rules are gone (fail-open). Worse: `active_config_path`/`resolve_config_path` still resolves to the broken file, so the first admin GUI mutation calls `persist_to_file` and atomically REPLACES the operator's original YAML with the serialized defaults — permanent loss of the config document. This directly contradicts the documented contract ('an unset ${env:NAME} with no default fails loudly at load'). The DGP_CONFIG branch (line 1595-1601) has the same fail-open behavior with only an eprintln warning; the search-path branch (1613-1619) is completely silent.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/config/mod.rs:1613 `if let Ok(config) = Self::from_file(path) { found = Some(config); break; }` discards the error with no logging; :1619 `found.unwrap_or_default()`. DGP_CONFIG branch :1595-1601 warns then `Self::default()`. Contrast src/main.rs:424 where explicit `--config` is a hard error (`Config::load_from_path(path)?`) — the no-flag path used in production (main.rs:426) fails open. Persist-over target: main.rs:684 `config_file_path = cli.config.clone().or_else(Config::resolve_config_path)` still returns the broken file's path, and persist_to_file (config/mod.rs:2523) rename-overwrites it.

confirmed_primary.title: apply_config_transition commits engine/IAM/snapshot side effects before the declarative-IAM gates can fail — a rejected apply leaves the runtime serving the NEW config while state.config and disk keep the OLD, response claims 'no state changed'
confirmed_primary.scenario: POST /config/apply (declarative iam_mode) with a doc that (a) changes storage — e.g. adds a bucket public_prefix or changes a backend — and (b) has an IAM defect not caught by parse_and_validate_yaml/Config::check (duplicate iam_users name, unknown group ref, `$`-prefixed name, access-key collision with a DB user — all only validated by diff_iam at step 4c; or simply the gui→declarative flip with empty IAM, or no config DB). Execution order: step 1 rebuilds and STORES the new engine (config_apply.rs:39 `app.engine.store(...)`), step 2 reloads the log filter, step 3 hot-swaps IamState for legacy-cred changes (mod.rs:307), step 4 publishes the new public-prefix snapshot + admission chain (mod.rs:319-321) — then step 4c fails (mod.rs:391/404/411-412) and returns Err. Both callers (document_level.rs:548-568, section_level.rs:611-635) return 422 'Config transition refused (no state changed)' WITHOUT swapping *cfg and WITHOUT rolling back any of steps 1-4. Result: the just-'rejected' public prefix is LIVE and anonymously readable (admission chain + prefix snapshot already published for the new buckets), the engine routes buckets per the new doc, while GET /config, the persisted file, and all subsequent diffs are computed against the old config. For an access-section PUT that changes legacy SigV4 creds + has invalid declarative IAM, the IamState swap at step 3 means S3 auth now requires the NEW keys while the config still shows the old ones. The doc comment on step 1 ('on failure the helper returns Err without touching any other state') is only true for step-0/1 failures.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/api/admin/config/mod.rs:260-262 (engine rebuild+store), :307 (iam_state.store), :319-321 (rebuild_bucket_derived_snapshots) all execute before the fallible returns at :391-397 (empty-YAML gate), :404-409 (missing config DB), :411-412 (reconcile Err), :421-422 (rebuild_iam_index Err). Callers do no rollback: src/api/admin/config/document_level.rs:550-568 returns 422 with error 'Config transition refused (no state changed)' and never executes `*cfg = incoming` (line 572); src/api/admin/config/section_level.rs:614-634 same pattern. diff_iam validation reachability: src/iam/declarative.rs:582-693 (duplicate names, group refs, reserved names, key collisions) — none of these are checked by Config::check (config/mod.rs:1653) or parse_and_validate_yaml (document_level.rs:233).

confirmed_primary.title: Declarative IAM reconcile permanently wipes OAuth provider client_secret on any redacted export→apply round-trip (users have a preservation shim, providers do not)
confirmed_primary.scenario: Deployment in iam_mode: declarative with an OIDC provider whose client_secret is a literal (in the DB from a prior reconcile or GUI-era setup). Operator follows the documented GitOps loop: GET /config/export (redact_all_secrets → redact_for_export sets `p.client_secret = None` unless it is an env ref, config/mod.rs:2377-2381), edits an unrelated field (e.g. a bucket quota), POST /config/apply. preserve_runtime_secrets (document_level.rs:352-392) restores bootstrap hash, SigV4 pairs, backend creds and webhook secrets — but NOT access.auth_providers. The reconciler then sees db.client_secret = Some(secret) vs yaml None → provider_equal false (declarative.rs:852) → providers_to_update pushes the YAML provider VERBATIM with client_secret: None (declarative.rs:747; unlike users, which go through desired_existing_user at :829-837 to keep the DB secret when YAML is empty). apply_iam_reconcile UPDATEs `client_secret = NULL` (config_db/declarative.rs:153-174). The OAuth client secret is destroyed in the encrypted DB and synced to all peers; the only trace is a 'providers updated: 1' stat. All OAuth/OIDC logins subsequently fail at token exchange. The same wipe happens through the access-section PUT (section GET redacts, merge-patch treats the auth_providers array as an atomic leaf). This violates the apply path's own stated contract ('for every secret that's None in the incoming doc, we copy the current runtime value across', document_level.rs:339-346).
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/iam/declarative.rs:744-750: `Some(dp) => diff.providers_to_update.push((dp.id, yp.clone()))` — no secret-preservation analog of desired_existing_user (:829-837 exists only for users). provider_equal at :852 compares `db.client_secret == yaml.client_secret`, so a redacted (None) yaml side always triggers the update. src/config_db/declarative.rs:157/168 writes the None straight into `client_secret = ?7`. Redaction source: src/config/mod.rs:2377-2381. Missing preservation: src/api/admin/config/document_level.rs:352-392 (preserve_runtime_secrets covers bootstrap hash, sigv4, backends, event_delivery — no auth_providers/iam_users) and src/api/admin/config/section_level.rs:356-417 likewise.

confirmed_primary.title: Hot-apply of codec_concurrency / max_passthrough_object_size reports success but has no effect: engine_affecting_fields_changed omits two fields the engine snapshots at construction
confirmed_primary.scenario: Operator raises advanced.max_passthrough_object_size from the 64 GiB default to 200 GiB via /config/apply or the section PUT to allow a large multipart upload. engine_affecting_fields_changed (mod.rs:183-196) compares backend/backends/buckets/max_object_size/cache_size_mb/max_delta_ratio/metadata_cache_mb only, so no engine rebuild happens; requires_restart_warnings (mod.rs:496-514) lists only listen_addr and cache_size_mb, so the response is applied:true, persisted:true, requires_restart:false, no warnings. But DeltaGliderEngine snapshots max_passthrough_object_size (engine/mod.rs:697) and codec_concurrency (engine/mod.rs:685, sizing the codec semaphore at :698) at construction — the running engine keeps the old 64 GiB limit and the 100+ GiB upload is still rejected (EntityTooLarge) despite the config API, the persisted YAML, and GET /config all showing the new value. The stale value silently heals only when some unrelated engine-affecting field changes or the process restarts. Same for codec_concurrency (stale xdelta3 parallelism cap).
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/api/admin/config/mod.rs:183-196 field list omits `max_passthrough_object_size` and `codec_concurrency`; its doc comment (:180-182) even claims everything outside the list is 'engine-orthogonal'. src/deltaglider/engine/mod.rs:685 (`config.codec_concurrency` → Semaphore at :698) and :697 (`max_passthrough_object_size: config.max_passthrough_object_size`, served via accessor at :871-872) prove both are engine-read. requires_restart_warnings at src/api/admin/config/mod.rs:496-514 covers neither, so no restart warning is emitted either.

confirmed_primary.title: persist_to_file does not re-escape `$` for the load-time env expander: runtime-entered values containing `$$` or a literal `${env:...}` are corrupted or mis-expanded on the next boot
confirmed_primary.scenario: Load applies `expand_env_vars` decoding to the raw file text (`$$`→`$`, `${env:NAME}`→env value or hard error, config/mod.rs:1334), but the persist path (persist_to_file → to_canonical_yaml_for_persist, :2502-2531) serializes the in-memory strings verbatim with no inverse `$`→`$$` encoding. Any string entered at RUNTIME (admin GUI/section PUT/document apply) that contains `$$` or a `${env:...}`-shaped substring round-trips wrong through persist→restart: (a) an admission Reject message or webhook header value `pay $$10` is persisted as-is and reloads as `pay $10`; (b) a bearer token or backend secret containing the substring `${env:HOME}` is persisted materialized, and on the next boot the expander substitutes the server's HOME into the credential (silent auth breakage), or — if the referenced name is unset — from_yaml_file returns MissingEnvVar, which via the silent-fallback in Config::load() (finding 1) boots the server on full defaults. The value the operator saved and the value served after restart differ with no warning.
confirmed_primary.evidence: Decode on load: src/config/mod.rs:1334 (`expand_env_vars_recording(&content)` in from_yaml_file) with `$$`→`$` at src/config/expansion.rs:109-111 and `${env:...}` substitution at :139-169. No encode on persist: to_canonical_yaml_for_persist (src/config/mod.rs:2502-2506) → with_env_refs_reinserted (:2183, only substitutes exact recorded values back to refs) → serde_yaml::to_string; grep shows no `$`→`$$` escaping anywhere in the persist pipeline. The asymmetry is total: every persisted document is re-decoded on the next from_yaml_file.

confirmed_primary.title: Declarative reconcile creates IAM users with empty secret_access_key — applying a redacted export to a fresh instance yields users whose SigV4 secret is the empty string
confirmed_primary.scenario: The canonical export redacts every iam_user secret to "" (config/mod.rs:2372-2376), and DeclarativeUser serializes secret_access_key unconditionally (declarative.rs:84-85), so the exported YAML carries `secret_access_key: ''` for every user. Bootstrapping a NEW instance from that export (fresh/empty config DB — the natural GitOps disaster-recovery flow): diff_iam's validate() (declarative.rs:582-693) has no empty-secret check, every user lands in users_to_create, and apply_iam_reconcile INSERTs them with secret_access_key = '' (config_db/declarative.rs:181-189). desired_existing_user's keep-DB-secret shim (:829-837) only protects UPDATEs of existing users — creates pass the empty string through. The IAM index then maps each access_key_id to an empty secret, and anyone who knows an access_key_id (a non-secret identifier that appears in the GitOps repo itself, logs, and audit entries) can compute valid SigV4 signatures with secret_key='' and authenticate as that user with their full permissions. No warning is emitted at validate, apply, or login.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/iam/declarative.rs:582-693 (validate: uniqueness, reserved names, group refs, permissions, key collisions — no non-empty-secret requirement); :774 `None => diff.users_to_create.push(yu.clone())` passes the empty secret verbatim (the :829-837 preservation shim only runs in the Some(du) update arm); src/config_db/declarative.rs:181-189 INSERTs `u.secret_access_key` unchecked. Export side: src/config/mod.rs:2372-2376 clears the secret to '' and src/iam/declarative.rs:84-85 (`#[serde(default)] pub secret_access_key: String`, no skip_serializing_if) guarantees the empty string is emitted in the YAML.

confirmed_primary.title: Concurrent same-node config-sync uploads self-conflict and the reconcile merge permanently deletes a just-committed IAM mutation
confirmed_primary.scenario: Admin creates user X then user Y in quick succession. Each mutation calls trigger_config_sync (src/api/admin/mod.rs:213-227), which tokio::spawn's an independent upload_with_reconcile — nothing serialises them. Interleaving: T1 (for X) reads the DB file BEFORE Y commits (blob = X only); T2 (for Y) reads the file after (blob = X+Y); both read the same expected_etag E (config_db_sync.rs:333); T1's PUT If-Match:E wins (remote now = X only, new etag E1); T2's PUT gets 412 → Conflict path resets last_etag to None and calls download_if_newer → downloads E1 (X only) → reopen_and_rebuild_iam → merge_iam_from DELETE-all+INSERT-all replaces the LOCAL IAM tables with the X-only blob (src/config_db/mod.rs:993-1002), deleting user Y from the live DB and rebuilding the IAM index without Y; T2's retry then uploads the X-only DB. User Y — for which the admin received 200 — is silently and permanently gone from every node, and its credentials stop authenticating. No peer node or concurrent edit to Y is required; the node loses its own committed write.
confirmed_primary.evidence: upload() (config_db_sync.rs:320-384) reads file then etag with no per-node upload mutex; ConfigDbSync has only an RwLock<Option<String>> etag and an AtomicBool (config_db_sync.rs:64-74). The conflict branch of upload_with_reconcile (config_db_sync.rs:741-775) unconditionally merges the downloaded remote over local via reopen_and_rebuild_iam → merge_iam_from, which is a whole-table replace (config_db/mod.rs:993-1002); nothing re-applies local rows newer than the downloaded blob before the retry re-reads and re-uploads the now-reverted file (config_db_sync.rs:321). The KNOWN-LIMIT comment (config_db_sync.rs:747-750) only covers a concurrent PEER mutation; here the clobber is self-inflicted by two spawned uploads from one node.

confirmed_primary.title: reopen_and_rebuild_iam skips ExternalAuthManager rebuild when the merged provider list is empty — a provider deleted on a peer keeps accepting logins on other nodes
confirmed_primary.scenario: Two-node HA deployment with one OIDC provider. The admin on node A deletes the provider (e.g. compromised IdP); A's DB syncs to S3. Node B's 5-minute poll downloads and merge_iam_from correctly empties B's auth_providers table — but the in-memory ExternalAuthManager rebuild is guarded by `if !providers.is_empty()`, so with zero providers the rebuild is skipped and B's manager keeps the deleted provider's live discovery/client config. OAuth logins through the removed provider continue to succeed on node B indefinitely (until a restart or until some OTHER provider exists to make the list non-empty). The exact operation the deletion was meant to stop keeps working on every node except the one where the admin clicked.
confirmed_primary.evidence: config_db_sync.rs:866-878: `let providers = db.load_auth_providers().unwrap_or_default(); if !providers.is_empty() { ext_auth.rebuild(&providers); ... }` — the empty case (the delete-last-provider sync) falls through without calling rebuild, leaving the previous provider set active in memory. This helper is the single path for startup sync, the periodic poll, sync-now, and conflict reconcile, so no other trigger heals it. Contrast: the IAM index (line 845-852) and session revocations (857-861) are unconditionally refreshed on the same merge.

confirmed_primary.title: With the shared S3 replication lease active, the local replication_state lease is never taken, making the lease-anchored delete-vs-run guard vacuous — a rule can be deleted in the acquire→begin_run gap while its run proceeds
confirmed_primary.scenario: config_sync_bucket is set (CAS-capable), so the scheduler is built with S3Lease. Scheduler tick: phase-2 acquire writes ONLY the S3 lease object (s3_lease.rs:207-238) — replication_state.leader_* stays NULL. In the window after the scheduler's post-acquire config re-check (scheduler.rs:132-146) but before the worker's begin_run inserts the 'running' history row, the operator calls DELETE on the rule: the H2 liveness guard (jobs.rs:833-841) checks replication_latest_run_status (no row yet) OR replication_lease_is_held / parity_lease_is_held (local tables — NULL because the lease lives in S3) → not blocked → the rule is removed from YAML and replication_reconcile_rules purges all its state/history/failure/parity rows. The worker then begin_run's and executes a full replication pass of a rule that no longer exists, re-inserting orphaned replication_run_history/failure rows keyed to a deleted rule (never cleaned until another delete/apply calls reconcile_rules), with the run invisible and un-cancellable from the Jobs UI (the rule row is gone).
confirmed_primary.evidence: replication_lease_is_held's own doc (state_store.rs:387-389) declares the lease 'the liveness anchor for delete-vs-run: a lease is taken BEFORE the run row exists, so a run-row check alone races the acquire-then-spawn gap' — but that invariant only holds for LocalLease (coordination/lease.rs:91-107 delegates to replication_try_acquire_lease) not S3Lease (coordination/s3_lease.rs writes only the S3 object; grep shows no local-table write). Scheduler acquire goes exclusively through the trait (scheduler.rs:103-112); the delete guard consults only the local tables (jobs.rs:835-841). The scheduler's config re-check (scheduler.rs:132-146) closes the race only when the delete commits BEFORE the re-check; a delete whose H2 check runs after the re-check but before begin_run passes unblocked.

confirmed_primary.title: Buffered delta GET falsely rejects legally stored objects when reference+delta exceeds max_object_size
confirmed_primary.scenario: Defaults: max_object_size=100MB, max_delta_ratio=0.75, spool_threshold=max_object_size. PUT app-v1.tar (80MB, delta-eligible) → becomes deltaspace reference (80MB). PUT app-v2.tar (85MB), xdelta3 delta = 25MB (ratio 0.29 < 0.75) → committed as delta, PUT returns 200. GET app-v2.tar: file_size 85MB ≤ spool_threshold(100MB) → buffered path → combined_size = 80MB(ref) + 25MB(delta) = 105MB > 100MB → EngineError::TooLarge → client gets EntityTooLarge on every GET, forever. Same for an object stored at exactly max_object_size (its self-delta GET fails: ref=100MB + delta > 100MB). Data is written and acknowledged but unreadable through the S3 API; CopyObject of it fails too (uses retrieve()).
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/deltaglider/engine/retrieve.rs:627-633: `let combined_size = reference.len() as u64 + delta.len() as u64; if combined_size > self.max_object_size { return Err(EngineError::TooLarge ...) }`. The store side has no matching gate: store.rs:663-664 commits any delta with `ratio < effective_ratio` (0.75 default), so r + d can be up to 1.75× max_object_size. The spooled path (no such guard) is only taken when `metadata.file_size > self.spool_threshold()` (retrieve.rs:148), which is `max_object_size` by default (retrieve.rs:177-179) — the failing objects are all ≤ that. The guard's comment claims reference+delta is 'a lower bound for the reconstructed object', which is false: the reconstruction is exactly file_size (≤ max); the reference is input, not output.

confirmed_primary.title: Stale-metadata-cache retry in retrieve_stream is dead code (error-variant mismatch); a stale cache entry produces persistent 404s and is kept alive by re-insert-on-read
confirmed_primary.scenario: Object k is cached as Passthrough; it is rewritten as Delta (store commit_delta_or_passthrough writes the delta then delete_passthrough_idempotent removes the passthrough file). A GET whose cache-miss resolve overlapped the PUT (resolve read old metadata from storage, then re-inserted it at retrieve.rs:60 AFTER store.rs:241's fresh insert) — or any second proxy instance, or the alias-key case below — now holds stale Passthrough metadata. Every subsequent GET: cache hit → get_passthrough_stream → StorageError::NotFound → `?` converts to EngineError::Storage(StorageError::NotFound) — the retry arm at retrieve.rs:95 matches only `EngineError::NotFound(_)`, so it NEVER fires: no invalidation, no fresh resolve, client gets NoSuchKey 404 for an object that exists. Worse, retrieve.rs:57-60 re-inserts the cached value on every hit, resetting the 10-min TTL — under continuous GET traffic the stale entry never expires. The key stays 404 until a HEAD/PUT/LIST-enrichment on it.
confirmed_primary.evidence: retrieve.rs:137-140 (`get_passthrough_stream(...).await?`), retrieve.rs:262-263 and 622 (`get_delta ... ?`) all surface missing storage as EngineError::Storage(StorageError::NotFound) via the `#[from]` conversion (engine/mod.rs:145-146); nothing in retrieve_with_metadata ever constructs EngineError::NotFound (only head()/delete()/try_unmanaged_passthrough map it explicitly). The retry arm `Err(EngineError::NotFound(_)) if from_cache` (retrieve.rs:95) is therefore unreachable, and api/errors.rs:231 maps Storage(NotFound) → NoSuchKey so the client sees a hard 404. Contrast: the range path's retry (retrieve.rs:484) matches `StorageError::NotFound` BEFORE conversion and works — the asymmetry confirms the intent. TTL refresh: retrieve.rs:57-60 inserts even when metadata came from the cache; moka time_to_live resets per insert (metadata_cache.rs:50).

confirmed_primary.title: Leading-slash keys alias to the same stored object but use distinct metadata-cache keys — PUT/DELETE via one alias never invalidates the other
confirmed_primary.scenario: Client PUTs key "foo" (v1, cached as "b/foo" via store.rs:241). A client with a path-join bug (double-slash URL, key "/foo") overwrites it with v2: ObjectKey::parse trims the leading '/' so storage writes the SAME object "foo", but store_inner invalidates/inserts under cache key "b//foo" (store.rs:97, raw `key`). GET "foo" now cache-hits stale v1 metadata: for a passthrough object the proxy streams v2 bytes with v1 Content-Length/ETag/sha (truncated or over-long response); for a delta object reconstruction yields v2 bytes vs v1 sha → ChecksumMismatch 500 — and per the dead-retry finding above, no recovery until TTL/HEAD. DELETE "/foo" likewise leaves the "b/foo" entry live.
confirmed_primary.evidence: types.rs:88 `let key = key.trim_start_matches('/');` makes "/foo", "//foo", "foo" the same physical object (validated_key operates on the trimmed parse). But every metadata-cache operation uses the caller's raw key: store.rs:97 and :241, retrieve.rs:48/60, engine/mod.rs:1288 (head insert), engine/mod.rs:1660 (delete invalidate). metadata_cache.rs:56-58 builds `"{bucket}/{key}"` verbatim, so the aliases occupy distinct entries that are never cross-invalidated. s3s passes `input.key` through unmodified (s3_adapter_s3s.rs:854, :677).

confirmed_primary.title: delete() removes only the newest storage variant — a leftover sibling variant resurrects deleted content
confirmed_primary.scenario: PUT k v1 lands as passthrough (poor ratio). PUT k v2 lands as delta; the best-effort `delete_passthrough_idempotent` cleanup fails transiently (S3 500) and is only warned (store.rs:734-742) — both variants now exist for k. DELETE k: resolve_metadata picks the newest (delta, mod.rs:1181), and the match at mod.rs:1618-1634 deletes ONLY that variant. The stale passthrough v1 survives; a subsequent GET k resolves it and serves v1 bytes with 200 instead of 404 — a deleted object resurrects with outdated content.
confirmed_primary.evidence: mod.rs:1618-1634 deletes exactly one variant based on `metadata.storage_info`; nothing deletes the sibling. The codebase already acknowledges the stale-sibling hazard for writes (put_delta_raw comment mod.rs:1027-1030: 'leaving it behind lets a later delta delete resurrect old content') but delete() itself doesn't apply the same defense — deleting both variants idempotently here would close it.

confirmed_primary.title: Migrate job resumed in phase 'flip' clears the write gate BEFORE the flip has actually happened — client writes to the still-source-routed bucket are lost (and deleted permanently when delete_source=true)
confirmed_primary.scenario: 1) Migrate job completes verify; migrate.rs:433-434 persists phase="flip" to the DB, THEN executes the actual config flip at migrate.rs:442-456. 2) Process crashes (SIGKILL/OOM/power) in that window — the config file still routes the bucket to the SOURCE backend. 3) On restart: boot re-arm uses maintenance_gate_arm_keys (store.rs:196-220), which for kind=migrate does `if !is_pre_flip(&phase) { continue; }` — and is_pre_flip (migrate.rs:83-85) classifies "flip" as POST-flip — so NO gate is armed for the bucket. 4) The requeued job is claimed; run_job computes post_flip_migrate=true (worker.rs:127) and CLEARS the gate (worker.rs:131) instead of arming it, then run_phases executes the flip with no drain. 5) Any client PUT to the bucket between reboot and the flip (a window of ≥60s lease-lapse + poll time) lands on the source backend, is never copied (copy phase already finished), becomes invisible after the flip, and — with delete_source=true — is PERMANENTLY DELETED by the cleanup sweep, which deletes every key reachable through the old-backend route (migrate.rs:533-541).
confirmed_primary.evidence: migrate.rs:433-434 (`phase = "flip"; persist(...)`) runs before the mutate_and_apply_strict at migrate.rs:442-456, so a crash leaves phase="flip" persisted with the flip NOT applied. worker.rs:127 `let post_flip_migrate = job.kind == "migrate" && !super::migrate::is_pre_flip(&job.phase);` treats "flip" as post-flip and clears the gate (worker.rs:129-132); store.rs:206-208 makes boot re-arm skip the same job. The comment at worker.rs:114-116 says the exception is for "a migrate resumed post-flip (cleanup)", but the code includes phase "flip", where the flip may not have happened yet. Re-arming for phase "flip" would be safe in the other crash ordering too (flip applied, phase not yet advanced): re-running the flip is idempotent and the gate is cleared at migrate.rs:461.

confirmed_primary.title: MaintenanceGate in-flight write counter leaks on client disconnect — subsequent maintenance jobs on the bucket fail the drain forever (until proxy restart)
confirmed_primary.scenario: A client starts a large PUT to bucket B; maintenance_gate_middleware calls gate.write_started("b") (gate.rs:171) and awaits next.run(request). The client kills the TCP connection mid-body; hyper drops the in-flight request/handler future, so gate.write_finished (gate.rs:173) never executes — the DashMap counter for "b" is permanently +1. Every later reencrypt/migrate job on bucket B then fails: drain_inflight_writes (worker.rs:484-502) polls until inflight==0, times out after DGP_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SECS (default 300s), and the job settles "failed" ("in-flight writes to 'b' did not drain"). Retrying the job fails identically; only a proxy restart clears the counter. The same leak exists in the admin bulk loops: run_copy_loop (src/api/admin/objects.rs:255/296), move_objects source-delete loop (objects.rs:410/451), bulk_delete (objects.rs:510/547) — an admin closing the browser mid-bulk-operation drops the handler future between write_started and write_finished. write_finished must run from a Drop guard, not straight-line code.
confirmed_primary.evidence: gate.rs:171-174: `gate.write_started(&bucket); let response = next.run(request).await; gate.write_finished(&bucket);` — no Drop guard, so cancellation of the future (the normal hyper behavior when the connection closes) skips the decrement. inflight_writes only ever decrements in write_finished (gate.rs:114-125). drain_inflight_writes at src/maintenance/worker.rs:492 loops `while state.maintenance_gate.inflight_writes(bucket) > 0` and returns Err at the deadline, failing the job (migrate stage phase at migrate.rs:266 and reencrypt at worker.rs:228 both call it).

confirmed_primary.title: MaintenanceGate::set_busy/clear are unsynchronized read-modify-write on ArcSwap — concurrent admin arm + worker clear can resurrect a cleared bucket into the busy set, leaving it 503-read-only indefinitely
confirmed_primary.scenario: The worker task finishes a job on bucket A and calls clear("a") (worker.rs:193) at the same instant an admin request creates a job for bucket B and calls set_busy("b") (api/admin/maintenance.rs:324). Both load the same snapshot {a}: clear computes {} and stores it; set_busy computes {a,b} and stores it LAST → final busy set is {a,b}. Bucket A — whose job is finished — is now permanently in the busy set: every client PUT/POST/DELETE to A gets 503 SlowDown (gate.rs:162-169) and admin bulk ops get 409, with no job to finish and nothing that will ever call clear("a") again (only a restart or a new job cycle on A fixes it). The opposite interleaving silently drops B's arm, breaking the documented "gate from creation — no create→claim window" invariant for migrate/reencrypt (mitigated by run_job's re-arm at claim, but the creation-to-claim window is ungated).
confirmed_primary.evidence: gate.rs:74-95: set_busy/clear do `let current = self.busy.load(); let mut next = HashSet::clone(&current); ...; self.busy.store(Arc::new(next));` — a classic lost-update on ArcSwap (no rcu/CAS loop, no mutex). Callers are genuinely concurrent: admin HTTP handlers (api/admin/maintenance.rs:166, 324, 328 arm; 378-380 clear on cancel-queued) vs the worker task (worker.rs:131, 135, 176, 193; migrate.rs:461-462). ArcSwap::store provides no atomicity for read-modify-write; `rcu` or a mutex is required.

confirmed_primary.title: Write-gate middleware TOCTOU: is_busy check and write_started increment are not atomic, so a write can be invisible to the worker's drain and land mid-rewrite, reverting client data
confirmed_primary.scenario: Request thread: evaluates gate.is_busy("b") → false (gate.rs:162), is preempted by the OS before reaching gate.write_started (gate.rs:171). Meanwhile the admin arm + worker sequence runs: set_busy("b"), then drain_inflight_writes reads inflight_writes("b")==0 (the preempted request never incremented) and the reencrypt job starts rewriting. The request thread resumes, increments, and executes a full PUT of new content over key K while the worker is doing retrieve(K-old)→store(K-old) — the client's new bytes are silently overwritten with stale bytes (the exact corruption the gate exists to prevent, per gate.rs:11-14). Window is a few instructions wide (no await between check and increment) but real on a multithreaded runtime under load; the canonical fix is increment-first, then check-and-reject (decrementing on rejection).
confirmed_primary.evidence: gate.rs:162-171: `if gate.is_busy(&bucket) { return SlowDown } gate.write_started(&bucket);` — the busy-check and the drain-visible increment are two separate atomic operations with no ordering against the worker's set_busy → drain sequence (worker.rs:135 then worker.rs:492). A request observed as not-busy but not yet counted is invisible to both mechanisms.

confirmed_primary.title: Migrate delete_source cleanup silently stops (job settles 'completed' with no note) when a sweep page contains only directory-marker keys
confirmed_primary.scenario: delete_source cleanup restarts its listing from the top each sweep and breaks when `deleted_this_sweep == 0` (migrate.rs:547). '/'-suffixed directory-marker keys are filtered out of deletion (migrate.rs:533) but still occupy listing slots and are never deleted. A source bucket whose first PAGE_SIZE (1000) keys are all directory markers (e.g. a deep folder tree created by a console that writes explicit "dir/" marker objects) yields a first sweep with deleted_this_sweep==0 and delete_failures==0 → break, note stays None → the job settles plain 'completed' while every real source object beyond the markers remains on the old backend, with no failure row and no operator note. The same filter also means marker objects themselves are always left behind on the source even in the normal case.
confirmed_primary.evidence: migrate.rs:533 filters `!k.ends_with('/')` so markers are never deleted and never counted; migrate.rs:547 `if page.next_continuation_token.is_none() || deleted_this_sweep == 0 { break 'cleanup; }` conflates "nothing left to delete" with "this page happened to contain nothing deletable"; the note logic at migrate.rs:561-578 only fires for cancel or delete_failures>0, so this exit is indistinguishable from full success.

confirmed_primary.title: Copy paths propagate stale dg-encrypted markers onto decrypted bodies — destination objects unreadable after encrypted→non-encrypting replication/migrate/lifecycle
confirmed_primary.scenario: Backend A has encryption mode aes256-gcm-proxy; backend B has mode none (or sse-kms/sse-s3, i.e. WriteMode::PassThrough). A replication rule (or bucket migrate job, or lifecycle transition) copies a passthrough object (e.g. photo.jpg) from a bucket on A to a bucket on B. copy_object_once's buffered path retrieves plaintext (source wrapper decrypts) but clones meta.user_metadata — which still contains dg-encrypted=aes-256-gcm-v1 and dg-encryption-key-id — and only removes request.strip_user_metadata_keys, which is &[] for replication (src/replication/worker.rs:883), lifecycle (src/lifecycle/worker.rs:766) and migrate (src/maintenance/migrate.rs:327). engine.store passes user_metadata through unchanged (store.rs:614), and the dest EncryptingBackend with no key / PassThrough mode returns the data verbatim WITHOUT touching metadata (encrypting.rs:935-944). Result: plaintext body stored at dest with encryption markers. verify_destination only HEADs size/multipart-etag, so the run reports success. Every subsequent GET of the dest object fails: since ALL backends are wrapped in EncryptingBackend even with mode none (engine/mod.rs:293-301), the read sees is_encrypted(meta)==true → pick_decrypt_key errors 'object was encrypted with key id X but this backend has NO encryption key configured' (encrypting.rs:995-1005), or for large sources copied via stream_copy_passthrough/spooled_copy the chunked marker survives (transfer.rs:325-334, 1061-1070) and the read fails with 'bad chunked-encryption magic'. The DR/mirror copy is silently unreadable exactly when it's needed.
confirmed_primary.evidence: transfer.rs:230-242 (buffered path: user_metadata = meta.user_metadata.clone(); only request.strip_user_metadata_keys removed — empty for replication/lifecycle/migrate), transfer.rs:325-334 (streamed multipart path, same), transfer.rs:1061-1070 (spooled path, same). Contrast the delta fast path which DOES call strip_encryption_markers(&mut meta.user_metadata) at transfer.rs:901 and :955 with the comment 'a surviving source marker on a re-encrypted or passed-through body is silent corruption on read', and the reencrypt job which strips explicitly via strip_user_metadata_keys: &[ENCRYPTION_MARKER_KEY, ENCRYPTION_KEY_ID_KEY] (src/maintenance/worker.rs:359). Dest write leaves markers intact when not encrypting: encrypting.rs:935-944 (encrypt_if_enabled returns data verbatim, metadata untouched, for PassThrough mode or key=None). Dest read then hard-fails: encrypting.rs:1029-1040 (decrypt_if_needed fires on the marker) → encrypting.rs:995-1015 (no-key / mismatch error), or encrypting.rs:1408-1419 chunked decode of a plaintext body. Every backend is wrapped so the marker is always interpreted: src/deltaglider/engine/mod.rs:293-301 and 449-452 ('Always wraps (even for mode:none)'). engine.retrieve/head do NOT strip the markers before exposing them (retrieve.rs:124-171 returns stored metadata; the delta gate at transfer.rs:802-811 depends on head carrying them), and engine.store passes user_metadata through (src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:614, :721, :901). No test covers encrypted-source → non-encrypting-dest copies (tests/delta_passthrough_test.rs:288 uses ONE shared key for both ends).

confirmed_primary.title: Pending outbox rows grow without bound when no consumer is active (default config), and enabling delivery later floods the entire backlog
confirmed_primary.scenario: Default production deployment: config DB present (bootstrap/IAM mode), event_delivery.enabled=false (the default), replication.enabled=false. Every successful PUT/DELETE/COPY/CompleteMultipartUpload appends a 'pending' row to event_outbox (src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:71-92 emit_object_event has no gate; src/api/handlers/object_helpers.rs:34-52 inserts unconditionally whenever config_db exists). dispatch_once returns immediately when !config.is_active() (event_delivery.rs:405-407) and the spawn loop `continue`s before it (line 382-385), so neither claiming NOR any pruner ever runs. All three pruners only delete status='delivered' or 'failed' rows (event_outbox.rs:558, 590, 626) — 'pending' rows are immortal. A busy proxy (CI artifact traffic, thousands of writes/day) grows the encrypted SQLCipher deltaglider_config.db forever (also bloating every config-sync S3 upload, which ships the whole DB blob). Second symptom: an operator who enables webhooks/Slack months later gets the ENTIRE historical backlog delivered as 'new' events (batch 500/tick) — the replication consumer deliberately seeds its cursor at MAX(id) to avoid exactly this replay (src/replication/event_consumer.rs:286-294), but the webhook dispatcher has no equivalent seed.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/api/handlers/object_helpers.rs:34-52 — enqueue_object_events inserts whenever state.config_db is Some, with no check that any consumer (delivery or replication) is enabled. src/event_delivery.rs:405-407 — `if !config.is_active() { return; }` skips claim AND all three prune calls (lines 469-498). src/event_outbox.rs:558/590/626 — every DELETE filters `status = 'delivered'` or `status = 'failed'`; no code path anywhere prunes 'pending' rows (grep for `DELETE FROM event_outbox` hits only these + purge_failed). Dispatcher spawned once at src/main.rs:770 with no backlog-seed on first activation.

confirmed_primary.title: truncate_error slices at a fixed byte offset — panics on a UTF-8 char boundary and permanently kills the delivery dispatcher
confirmed_primary.scenario: Operator configures Slack bot-token mode with unicode channel names (Slack allows them, e.g. slack_routes channels like '#развёртывание-прод') and the token expires → every channel fails → deliver_slack builds the aggregate error `slack chat.postMessage failed for all N channel(s): {channel}: {err}; ...` (event_delivery.rs:316-320) containing those channel names. With enough routes the string exceeds 1000 bytes; if byte index 1000 falls inside a multi-byte character, `&error[..1000]` panics ('byte index 1000 is not a char boundary'). Same trigger via a >1000-byte unicode glob pattern echoed by `invalid slack glob {p:?}` (slack_format.rs:27), which fails on EVERY delivery attempt. The panic occurs inside the tokio task spawned once at src/main.rs:770; the task dies and is never respawned — ALL webhook/Slack event delivery silently stops until process restart, while rows pile up in_progress/pending.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/event_delivery.rs:557-564 — `if error.len() <= MAX_ERROR_LEN { .. } else { format!("{}...", &error[..MAX_ERROR_LEN]) }` uses byte-length check then byte-index slicing, which panics off a char boundary. Error strings interpolate operator-controlled unicode: channel names at event_delivery.rs:307/316-320, glob patterns at src/slack_format.rs:27. Dispatcher loop lives inside the single tokio::spawn (event_delivery.rs:377-395); a panic terminates the JoinHandle with no supervisor/restart.

confirmed_primary.title: Slack incoming-webhook multi-endpoint delivery duplicates messages on partial failure — violates the module's own no-duplicate invariant
confirmed_primary.scenario: format=slack with webhook_url + webhook_urls pointing at two Slack Incoming Webhooks (channel A and channel B). Hook B is revoked (Slack returns 404 no_service). Loop posts to A (200, message lands in channel A), then B fails → returns Err → row is requeued pending with backoff. Every retry re-posts to A before failing on B again: with default max_attempts=8 (config_sections.rs:978-980), channel A receives the same notification up to 8 times. The bot-token path explicitly solved this exact problem with any_ok/partial-success handling and calls duplication 'CRITICAL' (event_delivery.rs:266-320); the incoming-webhook loop (and deliver_raw at lines 195-224, where at least the payload carries event.id for consumer-side dedupe — Slack messages carry nothing) has no equivalent, so an early-endpoint success is replayed on every retry.
