confirmed_primary.evidence: src/event_delivery.rs:337-360 — sequential `for endpoint in endpoints` returns Err on the first non-2xx AFTER earlier endpoints already received a 2xx; nothing records per-endpoint success, so dispatch_once (line 437-447) requeues the whole row and the next attempt re-posts to all endpoints. Contrast the bot-token path's any_ok partial-success logic at lines 278-320, whose comment states duplicates are unacceptable because Slack has no idempotency key.

confirmed_primary.title: webhook_urls REDACTED_SENTINEL restore is index-based — deleting/reordering a masked entry in exported YAML silently resurrects the removed secret URL and drops the kept one
confirmed_primary.scenario: IaC loop (a first-class flow: export → edit → /config/apply): current config has webhook_urls = [hookA, hookB] (both secret hooks.slack.com URLs). Export masks both to '__redacted__'. Operator deletes the FIRST entry (hookA was compromised/revoked) leaving webhook_urls: ['__redacted__'] and applies. preserve_event_delivery_secrets restores new[0] from old[0] → result is [hookA]: the revoked URL the operator deleted is silently kept receiving events, and hookB — the one they meant to keep — is dropped. Same corruption for any reorder of ≥2 masked entries. No warning is emitted; the applied config looks valid.
confirmed_primary.evidence: src/api/admin/config/mod.rs:622-630 — `for (i, url) in new.webhook_urls.iter_mut().enumerate() { if url == sentinel { if let Some(prev) = old.webhook_urls.get(i) { *url = prev.clone(); } } }` matches purely by position; the comment concedes 'index alignment holds for an untouched round-trip', but the export→hand-edit→apply path (document_level.rs:383 applies the same restore) has no untouched guarantee, unlike webhook_headers which are correctly restored by KEY (lines 597-607).

confirmed_primary.title: Signed AWS streaming-chunked PUTs (STREAMING-AWS4-HMAC-SHA256-PAYLOAD) fail with 501 when authentication is enabled
confirmed_primary.scenario: Deploy in bootstrap or IAM mode (auth enabled — the production default). A standard S3 client uploads with SigV4 per-chunk signing, i.e. header `x-amz-content-sha256: STREAMING-AWS4-HMAC-SHA256-PAYLOAD` (or `...-TRAILER`) — the default for many AWS SDKs / aws-cli operations. The SigV4 middleware (src/api/auth.rs:894-896) inserts `SignedPayloadHash("STREAMING-AWS4-HMAC-SHA256-PAYLOAD")` into request extensions. In `put_object`, `verify_signed_payload_hash_s3s(signed_payload_hash.as_ref(), &data)` at s3_adapter_s3s.rs:797 calls `SignedPayloadHash::verify_against_body`, which for any value starting with `STREAMING-AWS4-HMAC-`/`STREAMING-AWS4-ECDSA-` returns `Err(NotImplemented)` (auth.rs:192-196 via `requires_chunk_signature_verification`). That maps to a 501 NotImplemented — the upload is rejected. The body was already fully decoded by `collect_blob_limited` (aws-chunked framing stripped), so the ONLY reason it fails is the sentinel re-check. The integration tests in tests/aws_chunked_upload_test.rs cover exactly this variant and pass — but only because they run against an open-access (auth-none) TestServer, where the SigV4 middleware never inserts `SignedPayloadHash`, so `verify_signed_payload_hash_s3s` is never invoked. There is no test exercising a signed streaming PUT with auth on.
confirmed_primary.evidence: put_object unconditionally re-verifies via s3_adapter_s3s.rs:797; verify_against_body rejects signed-streaming sentinels (src/api/auth.rs:191-196). SignedPayloadHash is inserted for every authenticated request regardless of streaming (src/api/auth.rs:894-896, capturing the raw x-amz-content-sha256 = 'STREAMING-...'). The differential: the identical upload succeeds with auth off (tests/aws_chunked_upload_test.rs:194-214 asserts success) but 501s with auth on. STREAMING-UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD-TRAILER escapes (doesn't match the prefix → is_verifiable_hex()==false → Ok), so only the SIGNED streaming variants break — a client whose SDK signs chunks cannot upload.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Buffered-encode stdout cap (max_stdout = max_object_size) hard-errors a valid PUT that should fall back to passthrough — a second dissimilar large delta-eligible object into an existing deltaspace returns 5xx instead of storing
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Default config: max_object_size=100MB, spool_store_threshold defaults to max_object_size, so any delta-eligible object with data.len() <= 100MB takes the BUFFERED path (s3_adapter_s3s.rs:828 routes to store_spooled_delta only when data.len() > spool_store_threshold, i.e. strictly >100MB). Sequence: (1) PUT A = a 99MB delta-eligible file (e.g. releases/v1/a.bin) to prefix p/ — first member self-deltas against its own fresh reference (identical → tiny delta), committed fine, A becomes the 99MB reference. (2) PUT B = a 99MB UNRELATED delta-eligible file (releases/v1/b.bin, dissimilar bytes) to the same prefix p/. store_inner → encode_and_store → codec.encode(reference=99MB A, target=99MB B). xdelta3 with -D emits ADD-covered output for the unrelated target ≈ 99MB + VCDIFF window/instruction framing, which exceeds the max_stdout cap = self.max_size = max_object_size = 100MB (the 99MB target plus overhead crosses the ~1MB headroom). pipe_stdin_stdout_stderr's stdout_reader (codec.rs:220-228) reads take(max_stdout+1), sees buf.len() > max_stdout, and returns Err(std::io::Error 'output exceeds maximum size'). run_xdelta3 propagates this as CodecError::EncodeFailed. encode_and_store does `codec.encode(...)??` (store.rs:276) so the error surfaces BEFORE commit_delta_or_passthrough ever runs — the ratio-lost→passthrough fallback (store.rs:664) is never reached. store_inner's error branch (store.rs:216-239) does NOT convert to passthrough; with has_existing_reference=true it simply returns Err(e). PUT B fails with a 5xx even though B is a perfectly valid, storable object that should have been kept as passthrough. Deterministic under unchanged default config for any second dissimilar object whose size is close enough to max_object_size that delta+overhead > max_object_size. The STREAMING path does NOT have this bug: store_spooled_delta uses a CapWriter at cap = size×ratio and treats cap-exceeded as Ok(None) = 'passthrough wins' (store.rs:500, 514-544) — graceful. Only the buffered encode path treats an oversized delta as a hard error. This is the exact sibling of the reported 'buffered GET pre-decode guard rejects legitimately stored objects' bug, on the PUT side.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: codec.rs:220-228 caps buffered encode/decode stdout at max_stdout and returns an IO error on exceed; run_xdelta3 passes max_stdout = self.max_size (codec.rs:643) which equals max_object_size (engine/mod.rs:693 DeltaCodec::new(config.max_object_size)). encode_and_store (store.rs:276) does codec.encode(...)?? — the codec error short-circuits before the ratio decision in commit_delta_or_passthrough (store.rs:663-703). store_inner's failure branch (store.rs:216-239) returns Err(e), never falling back to passthrough. Contrast store_spooled_delta's CapWriter which returns Ok(None)→passthrough on cap-exceed (store.rs:499-544). Buffered routing is the default: s3_adapter_s3s.rs:828 only spools when data.len() > spool_store_threshold (strict), and spool_store_threshold defaults to max_object_size (engine/mod.rs:808-810).

confirmed_adjacent.title: S3Backend::head_bucket collapses transient errors (503 SlowDown / connect reset) into Ok(false) → every bucket-gated verb returns spurious NoSuchBucket during a backend blip
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Single-backend S3/MinIO/Hetzner deployment. Bucket 'releases' exists. The backend briefly 503-SlowDown-throttles (the exact Hetzner throttle storm the code elsewhere has an RCA for, s3.rs:214/292/418) or drops a connection. A client PUT/GET/DELETE/LIST/HeadBucket arrives. The handler calls ensure_bucket_exists_s3s (or the PutObject gate at s3_adapter_s3s.rs:787) → engine.head_bucket → S3Backend::head_bucket. The HeadBucket SDK call returns a transient Err; line 1309 `Err(_) => Ok(false)` reports it as 'bucket absent'. ensure_bucket_exists_s3s (s3_adapter_s3s.rs:1442-1453) then returns `NoSuchBucket` to the client — a hard, non-retryable 404 for a bucket that plainly exists. This fans out across ~15 verbs (HeadBucket:114, GetObject/ListObjectsV2/DeleteObject/DeleteObjects/tagging/CopyObject/CreateMultipartUpload:415-1308) and hits DGP's own replication/lifecycle engine PUTs too. Unlike routing bug #1, NO same-name collision and NO RoutingBackend is required — it fires on the common single-backend path.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: s3.rs:1306-1311 `match ... { Ok(_) => Ok(true), Err(_) => Ok(false) }` — the ONLY match arm for Err collapses 404, 403, 503 SlowDown, and transport errors identically. The dedicated classifier `classify_s3_error` (s3.rs:366) already distinguishes BucketNotFound (404/403) from Throttled (503/SlowDown) and is used by every other S3 op, but head_bucket ignores it. head_bucket also has no `with_retry` wrapper (unlike put/get). Callers trust Ok(false) as definitive absence: ensure_bucket_exists_s3s (s3_adapter_s3s.rs:1448-1452) and the PutObject gate (s3_adapter_s3s.rs:787-793) both map false → `s3_error!(NoSuchBucket)`.

confirmed_adjacent.title: resolve_existing is re-invoked per storage call within a single store()/retrieve(), so a mid-operation default-backend head_bucket flap can split one deltaspace's reference.bin and delta across two same-named buckets on different backends
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Multi-backend config where 'backups' exists on BOTH the default backend (hetzner) and a secondary (aws-dr) — the same name-collision precondition as confirmed bug #1, but the consequence here is intra-operation split rather than whole-request misroute. A client PUT to 'backups' enters Engine::store, which makes SEPARATE routed calls: has_reference (store.rs:403), get_reference/get_reference_to_file, then put_delta/put_reference. Each independently calls RoutingBackend::resolve_existing (routing.rs:223), which does `default.head_bucket(bucket).await.unwrap_or(false)` (line 232). If the default backend's head_bucket succeeds for the reference read but transiently errors (503) for the subsequent delta write — or vice versa — the two halves resolve to DIFFERENT backends (hetzner vs aws-dr). The delta is written against a reference.bin that lives in a different physical bucket. A later GET reconstructs the delta against the wrong/absent reference → corrupt or unreadable object. The per-deltaspace prefix_lock (store.rs:140/172/402) does NOT prevent this: it serializes concurrent same-prefix ops on one node but never pins the resolved backend across the calls of a single operation.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: routing.rs:232 and 243 (`unwrap_or(false)`) turn any head_bucket error into 'absent', and resolve_existing is called fresh by each StorageBackend method (route_existing! macro, routing.rs:252-257) — there is no per-operation backend cache. Engine::store issues ≥2 routed storage calls per PUT (has_reference at store.rs:403 + the delta/reference writes), each re-resolving. The confirmed routing bug #1 describes a whole-request misroute; this is the distinct escalation where a single logical write is split mid-flight, defeating the deltaspace layout invariant (bucket/prefix/.dg/reference.bin + delta must co-reside).

confirmed_adjacent.title: verify's in-progress-run guard is bypassed under a coordination bucket → parity audit runs against a mid-sync destination and reports a false 'not in sync' verdict
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: config_sync_bucket is configured. A scheduled replication run of rule R is in flight, holding ONLY the S3 coordination lease (scheduler.rs:103-112, S3Lease). Operator (or the UI's run-now→verify flow) POSTs verify for R. The guard at replication.rs:308-320 checks db.replication_lease_is_held(&rule.name) which reads the node-local SQLite replication_state lease (state_store.rs:390-402) — the scheduler's S3 lease is invisible to it, so the check returns false, the 409 is NOT returned, and parity_audit proceeds. It scans a destination that is actively being written by the scheduled run and returns a spurious 'not in sync' verdict — exactly the race the finding-#17 guard was added to prevent. The comment at replication.rs:305-307 states this guard's purpose but it only holds when the run is a node-local (run-now / no-coordination) run.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: replication.rs:311 db.replication_lease_is_held is SQLite-only (state_store.rs:390 → job_store::lease_is_held on replication_state). A scheduler run in coordination mode never writes that SQLite lease (scheduler.rs uses lease.try_acquire = S3Lease). Same disjoint-store root cause as the event-consumer finding.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Parity mismatch remediation on S3/encrypting (Transforming) rules uses S3 LastModified as created_at, never the DG created_at from the resolving HEAD → newer-wins remediation predicts the wrong winner and falsely promises 're-run fixes this'
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: For an S3 (or encrypting) source/dest — the Transforming regime — a ChecksumMismatch key is HEAD-resolved by resolve_logical / resolve_logical_dest. apply_logical (parity.rs:636-647) overlays sha256/size/etag/multipart_parts from the HEAD but does NOT overlay created_at, and cache_entry_from_meta (parity.rs:653-665) never stores created_at either. So ObjState.created_at stays whatever ObjState::from_metadata set from the LITE list (parity.rs:224), which on S3 is obj.last_modified — the S3 LastModified — not the DG-managed created_at (lite_metadata_from_listed, s3.rs:1178-1185 passes last_modified into FileMetadata::fallback's created_at slot). annotate_findings then feeds those LastModified-derived timestamps into analyze_mismatch_newer_wins (remediation.rs:205-250), which forks on src_created_at vs dst_created_at. But the replication planner's actual newer-wins copy decision uses the DG created_at from a HEAD (planner.rs:310 `src_meta.created_at > dest.created_at`). Concrete: DG created_at src=100(old), dst=200(dst independently rewritten) → planner SKIPs (dest newer). S3 LastModified src=500, dst=300 → remediation sees src>dst → emits reason SourceModifiedAfterCopy, rerun_helps:Yes, FixAction::RunNow ('re-run the rule — newer-wins copies the newer source'). Operator re-runs; planner uses DG created_at (100<200) → SKIP. The remediation lied about re-run fixing it — same fabricated-guidance class as the reported bug, in the mismatch/NewerWins path.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: apply_logical (parity.rs:636-647) overlays sha256/size/etag/multipart_parts only — no created_at; created_at is set solely at parity.rs:224 from the lite metadata; S3 lite metadata carries obj.last_modified as created_at (s3.rs:1164-1185, FileMetadata::fallback), which is NOT the DG created_at a HEAD returns; annotate_findings passes source/dest ObjState.created_at into FindingFacts (parity.rs:516-517) consumed by analyze_mismatch_newer_wins (remediation.rs:206-250), whereas the planner's real decision uses HEAD-sourced DG created_at (planner.rs:310). No test exercises the S3-LastModified vs DG-created_at divergence — all created_at tests use filesystem metadata.

confirmed_adjacent.title: CopyObject source-read auth drops request context — IP/condition-scoped Deny (or Allow) on the source is silently ignored, letting a user copy-exfiltrate objects they are forbidden to read directly
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: IAM user policy = `Allow read on secrets/*` PLUS `Deny read on secrets/* IF aws:SourceIp NotIpAddress 10.0.0.0/8` (read secrets only from the corporate LAN) PLUS `Allow write on pub/*`. From external IP 1.2.3.4 the user sends `PUT /pub/leak` with header `x-amz-copy-source: /secrets/customer-keys`. (a) authorization_middleware only sees the DEST path `/pub/leak` → classifies Write on pub/leak, evaluated with aws:SourceIp=1.2.3.4 → user has Allow write pub/* → admitted; the middleware never sees the source. (b) copy_object then calls check_copy_source_access_s3s(user,"secrets","customer-keys") → user.can(Read,"secrets","customer-keys") at line 1541, which is CONTEXT-FREE: it calls evaluate_iam with `&Default::default()` (empty Context). aws:SourceIp is MISSING, so in iam-rs 0.0.23 the NotIpAddress condition returns if_exists=false (operator_eval.rs:242/272/322) → the Deny does NOT fire → the `Allow read secrets/*` matches → can() returns true → source read is ALLOWED. The user reads secrets/customer-keys from a forbidden IP and copies it into a public bucket. A direct `GET /secrets/customer-keys` from 1.2.3.4 IS blocked by the middleware (can_with_context includes aws:SourceIp → Deny fires → 403), so the copy path is strictly weaker and defeats the admin's IP carve-out. The inverse also breaks legitimately: a `Allow read IF aws:SourceIp IpAddress 10.0.0.0/8` grant false-DENIES copies from inside the LAN because the missing context makes the conditioned Allow evaluate to not-applicable.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:1541 `if user.can(S3Action::Read, source_bucket, source_key)` calls AuthenticatedUser::can (src/iam/types.rs:141-147) which passes `&Default::default()` as the context to evaluate_iam. AuthenticatedUser (src/iam/types.rs:129) has no source-IP/context field, and the adapter never constructs a Context — contrast the middleware (src/iam/middleware.rs:144-159) which deliberately injects aws:SourceIp so IP-conditioned policies fire. iam-rs missing-context semantics: src/evaluation/operator_eval.rs:242 `None => Ok(if_exists)` (false for non-IfExists operators like NotIpAddress). check_copy_source_access_s3s is called from copy_object (line 890) and upload_part_copy (line 1299), so both copy variants are affected.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Presigned form-POST per-key write check drops request context — an IP/condition-scoped Deny write on the target prefix is silently ignored for browser multipart/form-data uploads
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: IAM user policy = `Allow write on uploads/*` PLUS `Deny write on uploads/quarantine/* IF aws:SourceIp NotIpAddress 10.0.0.0/8`. A browser multipart/form-data PostObject (intercepted by intercept_form_post_for_s3s BEFORE the s3s fallback, so it does NOT pass through authorization_middleware's context-aware check) targets key `uploads/quarantine/payload.bin` from external IP 1.2.3.4. authorize_form_post calls auth_user.can(S3Action::Write, bucket, "uploads/quarantine/payload.bin") at line 695 — context-free. aws:SourceIp is absent, the conditioned Deny evaluates to if_exists=false and does not fire, the `Allow write uploads/*` matches → the write is admitted, bypassing the IP-scoped Deny the admin authored. The equivalent SigV4 PUT through the normal middleware would be blocked (can_with_context includes aws:SourceIp). Lower severity than the copy/delete paths because the form-POST surface is browser-only and additionally policy-signature gated, but it is the same context-blind per-key defect.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: src/api/handlers/form_post.rs:695 `if !auth_user.can(S3Action::Write, bucket, &parsed.resolved_key)` uses the context-free can(). The form-POST path is intercepted at startup (src/startup.rs:722-725 intercept_form_post_for_s3s) ahead of the s3s fallback and does its own IAM check here rather than relying on authorization_middleware, so the aws:SourceIp context the middleware injects (src/iam/middleware.rs:144-159) is never applied to this decision. Same iam-rs missing-context semantics (operator_eval.rs:242).

confirmed_adjacent.title: Idle-TTL sweeper (cleanup_expired) deletes relay part files of an actively-Completing large upload, causing store failure and permanent part loss on retry
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: A client does a passthrough multipart upload that promotes to relay mode (parts written under /tmp/deltaglider-mpu-relay/<upload_id>/). On CompleteMultipartUpload, complete_passthrough (src/multipart.rs:526) flips the upload to Completing, sets last_activity=now once, and returns the relay part paths. The adapter then calls engine.store_passthrough_relayed_parts_with_multipart_etag (src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:1167-1179), which streams every part file to compute hashes and then hands the paths to put_passthrough_parts — for the S3 backend this re-reads all parts (default trait impl, src/storage/traits.rs:272-287). For a hundred-GB relayed object to a slow backend this store can run longer than completing_timeout. The periodic sweeper (src/main.rs:552, every 300s) calls cleanup_expired: for a Completing upload it checks ONLY `u.last_activity <= completing_cutoff` (multipart.rs:944-946) with completing_cutoff = now - completing_timeout (default DGP_MULTIPART_COMPLETING_TIMEOUT_SECS = 3600s, operator-tunable down to 1s). There is NO 'store in progress' flag distinct from Completing and NO heartbeat updating last_activity during the store — so once the fixed timeout elapses from the ONE last_activity stamp, the sweeper removes the upload from the map and cleanup_relay_dir_for_upload (multipart.rs:966,1066-1070) does fs::remove_dir_all on the relay dir whose files the store is still reading. The in-flight put_passthrough_parts / hash loop then hits tokio::fs::read/File::open on a deleted path → StorageError → EngineError → the adapter runs rollback_upload (s3s_adapter:1184) returning the upload to Open. But the relay files are now permanently gone: the client's retried CompleteMultipartUpload re-invokes complete_passthrough → ordered_relay_part_paths returns the same (deleted) paths → store re-reads deleted files → fails again forever. The client must re-upload every part (tens of GB) — identical data-loss consequence to the confirmed orphan-sweep bug, but reached via a DIFFERENT sweeper (cleanup_expired vs sweep_orphan_relay_artifacts) and a DIFFERENT trigger (a legitimately slow store exceeding completing_timeout, not a promotion race or partial promote).
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: cleanup_expired's Completing branch (src/multipart.rs:943-954) sweeps purely on last_activity age with no protection for an in-flight store, and cleanup_relay_dir_for_upload (966) unconditionally remove_dir_all's the relay directory. complete_passthrough stamps last_activity exactly once at flip time (multipart.rs:574) and nothing updates it during store_passthrough_relayed_parts_with_multipart_etag, whose read loop (src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:948-961) plus put_passthrough_parts re-read (traits.rs:272-287) can exceed the default 3600s / operator-lowered completing_timeout (src/main.rs:516-523, .max(1)). ordered_relay_part_paths (multipart.rs:1045-1063) returns the same deterministic part-{:05}.bin paths on the client's retry, so once deleted the retry can never validate.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Document-level /config/apply silently strips per-backend encryption keys on any redacted export→apply round-trip — historical objects become unreadable and new writes land unencrypted
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Deployment has a backend with `backend_encryption` (or `backends[i].encryption`) mode aes256-gcm-proxy whose `key` is a literal hex in the running config (config-file-provisioned, not env). Operator follows the documented GitOps loop: GET /config/export (redact_for_export → BackendEncryptionConfig::redact_secrets clears key + legacy_key to None, config/mod.rs:2361-2364), edits an unrelated field, POST /config/apply. `parse_and_validate_yaml` does NOT reapply env overrides (apply_backend_encryption_env runs only in load-time apply_env_overrides, config/mod.rs:1534-1539), so the key is not refilled. `preserve_runtime_secrets` (document_level.rs:352-392) restores bootstrap hash, SigV4 pairs, backend SigV4 creds and event_delivery secrets — but NEVER calls preserve_backend_encryption_secrets, so the encryption key stays None. apply_config_transition rebuilds the engine; wrap_backend_with_encryption hits the `Aes256GcmProxy { key: None }` arm (engine/mod.rs:510-517) which only warns and builds a backend that writes UNENCRYPTED (WriteMode::Encrypt with key=None = passthrough) AND has no legacy_key. Result: every historical object encrypted with the now-gone key becomes permanently unreadable ('object encrypted, no key configured'), and all new writes are stored in plaintext while the config/UI/persisted YAML still claim aes256-gcm-proxy. `Config::check` emits a warning about the missing key but the apply still returns applied:true and commits.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: document_level.rs:352-392 preserve_runtime_secrets restores bootstrap/SigV4/event_delivery but has no backend-encryption preservation (the section path does: section_level.rs:397-412 calls preserve_backend_encryption_secrets for the singleton + each named backend). redact_for_export strips the keys (config/mod.rs:2361-2364). engine/mod.rs:510-517 builds an unencrypted, no-legacy-key backend from key:None. parse_and_validate_yaml (document_level.rs:233-274) never reapplies env overrides. This is the exact class as the confirmed OAuth-provider-secret wipe (finding #3), on a parallel write path the fix missed.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Declarative reconcile creates OIDC providers with NULL client_secret when a redacted export is applied to a fresh instance — every OAuth provider silently fails to load
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: GitOps disaster-recovery: a redacted config export (redact_for_export sets every auth_provider.client_secret = None unless it is an env ref, config/mod.rs:2377-2381) is applied to a NEW instance with an empty config DB (iam_mode: declarative). In diff_iam, each YAML provider is absent from the DB so it lands in `providers_to_create` verbatim — `yp.clone()` (iam/declarative.rs:748) with no secret-preservation shim (unlike users, which... also lack a create-time shim, and unlike existing-provider updates which have none either). apply_iam_reconcile INSERTs `client_secret` = NULL (config_db/declarative.rs:130-148, param p.client_secret). `validate` (declarative.rs:582-693) never checks for empty/missing client_secret. At index-rebuild, ExternalAuthState skips any provider whose client_secret is None ('missing client_id, client_secret, or issuer_url', iam/external_auth/mod.rs:55-71). Net: the reconcile reports 'providers created: N', but zero OIDC providers actually load and all OAuth/OIDC logins are impossible, with no error at validate/apply/login. Distinct from the confirmed provider-UPDATE-wipe (finding #3): this is the CREATE path on a fresh DB, the natural DR flow.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: declarative.rs:744-749 pushes new providers verbatim (no keep-DB-secret shim; the desired_existing_user shim at :829-837 only fires on the UPDATE branch and only for users). config_db/declarative.rs:130-148 INSERTs p.client_secret directly. iam/external_auth/mod.rs:55-71 skips providers with None client_secret at load. Same class as findings #3 and #6 (empty-secret create), applied to auth_providers.

confirmed_adjacent.title: with_env_refs_reinserted rewrites any runtime string equal to a recorded env-ref value into ${env:NAME}, coupling/corrupting an unrelated secret across persist and restart
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Config has a ref-sourced field, e.g. `access.iam_users[0].secret_access_key: ${env:SHARED}` with env SHARED=hunter2, so env_refs records SHARED→hunter2. Operator later sets a DIFFERENT field to the literal value hunter2 via the GUI/section PUT (e.g. a webhook bearer header, a backend secret, or another user's key that happens to equal that value). On persist/export, with_env_refs_reinserted builds a value→ref inverse map (config/mod.rs:2189-2197) and walks EVERY string scalar (walk, :2199-2218), rewriting the unrelated field's literal hunter2 into `${env:SHARED}` because it only matches by value, not by provenance/field-path. On the next boot the expander resolves that field to whatever SHARED currently is — if SHARED was rotated, the two now silently diverge from what the operator saved; if SHARED is later unset, from_yaml_file returns MissingEnvVar (and per confirmed finding #1 the search-path loader then boots on full defaults). The value the operator entered and the value served after restart differ with no warning.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: config/mod.rs:2189-2197 keys the inverse map purely on the value string; :2199-2218 walk() blindly substitutes any matching scalar anywhere in the tree with no field-path or provenance check. Same env-ref round-trip corruption class as confirmed finding #5 (persist not re-escaping $), on the reinsertion side.

confirmed_adjacent.title: field-level PATCH claims success:true and asserts 'engine was left untouched' after apply_config_transition fails at a post-engine step (declarative reconcile / snapshot), leaving runtime on the new config while cfg/disk revert to old
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: A PATCH sets iam_mode:declarative (field_level.rs:688-690) together with any engine-affecting or bucket/admission change. apply_config_transition runs step 1 engine rebuild (STORES the new engine, config_apply.rs), step 3 IamState swap, step 4 public-prefix + admission snapshot publish (mod.rs:319-321) — then step 4c declarative reconcile fails (empty-YAML gate, missing config DB, or diff_iam validation error, mod.rs:391/404/411). It returns Err. The handler's Err arm (field_level.rs:718-733) restores *cfg = old_cfg on the COMMENTED-FALSE assumption that 'The engine was left untouched by the helper (failure happens before it's stored)' — but the engine, iam_state, admission chain, and public-prefix snapshot were already swapped to the new config. The response is `success: true` with only a warning. Result: the engine routes buckets / a just-'rejected' public prefix is LIVE and anonymously readable, while GET /config, the persisted YAML, and subsequent diffs are all computed against the reverted old config — a permanent runtime-vs-config split-brain, reported to the operator as SUCCESS. This is the field-level manifestation of confirmed finding #2 (which named document_level and section_level), and is arguably worse because it returns success:true and its rollback comment is provably wrong.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: field_level.rs:697 calls apply_config_transition; :718-727 Err arm rolls back only *cfg with the comment 'The engine was left untouched by the helper (failure happens before it's stored)'. apply_config_transition (mod.rs:260-262 engine store, :307 iam_state.store, :319-321 snapshot publish, :391/404/411 reconcile Err) proves side effects commit before the failing step. Response is success:true (:712-716, :728-732).

confirmed_adjacent.title: TLS and config_sync_bucket changes via /config/apply report requires_restart:false and are silently ignored — no hot-apply, no restart warning
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Operator enables TLS or rotates a cert (sets advanced tls.enabled/cert_path/key_path) OR turns on multi-instance sync (sets config_sync_bucket) via POST /config/apply or a section PUT. Both fields are documented engine-orthogonal (mod.rs:181-182) so apply_config_transition never rebuilds anything for them, and TLS (bound once at startup, tls.rs) / the config-sync poller (launched once at startup, startup.rs:1266/1415) are never re-read. requires_restart_warnings (mod.rs:496-514) checks ONLY listen_addr and cache_size_mb, so the response is applied:true, persisted:true, requires_restart:false, warnings:[] . The running server keeps serving plaintext HTTP / never starts the sync poller, while the config API, persisted YAML, and GET /config all show the new value — the operator believes TLS is on / sync is enabled when it is not, and is not told a restart is required. Same class as confirmed finding #4 (silent no-op reported as success), for TLS and config_sync_bucket instead of max_passthrough_object_size/codec_concurrency.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: requires_restart_warnings (mod.rs:496-514) enumerates only listen_addr + cache_size_mb. The doc block at mod.rs:181-182 lists tls config and config_sync_bucket as engine-orthogonal (no rebuild). startup.rs:1266/1415 read config_sync_bucket only at boot to launch the poller; tls.rs binds TLS at startup. Neither is hot-applied nor flagged.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Config-sync poll REPLACES the in-memory session-revocation map, silently un-revoking a compromised key/cookie that was revoked-in-memory-first but whose DB write races the poll
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Admin revokes a compromised access key K on node A (POST /api/admin/sessions/revoke-user). revoke_identities_everywhere (src/api/admin/sessions.rs:85-86) FIRST records the revocation in memory via note_revocation(K, now) — deliberately before any lock, so the node 'fails closed immediately' — THEN acquires the ConfigDb mutex and persists K to the session_revocations table (line 91-98). Concurrently the 5-minute config-sync poll (src/startup.rs:1530) fires, acquires the same ConfigDb mutex FIRST, runs reopen_and_rebuild_iam → merge_iam_from (a peer blob that does not yet carry K's revocation, because A's push hasn't happened), then calls sessions.set_revocations(load_session_revocations()) at src/config_db_sync.rs:858-860. set_revocations does `*self.revocations.write() = rows.into_iter().collect()` (src/session.rs:169) — a full REPLACE, not a monotonic MAX-merge like note_revocation. Interleave: (T0) revoke does note_revocation(K) into memory (no lock held); (T1) poll wins the DB mutex, merges a peer blob lacking K, set_revocations REPLACES the in-memory map → K's revocation is WIPED from memory; (T2) revoke finally takes the mutex and writes K to the DB. Net state: DB has K, but node A's in-memory revocations map does NOT (it was overwritten at T1 and never re-asserted — the revoke handler returns 200 'revoked' and nothing re-runs note_revocation). entry_live (src/session.rs:194-200) reads only the in-memory map, so every existing session / stolen cookie for compromised key K continues to authenticate successfully on node A for up to 5 minutes (until the next poll reloads from the now-K-carrying DB). The exact escape hatch the revoke exists to provide is defeated on the node the admin clicked on. A second, DB-independent trigger for the same wipe: if the DB persist at src/api/admin/sessions.rs:94 fails transiently (persisted=false), memory holds K but the DB never does, so the very next poll's set_revocations wipes K from memory permanently (until restart). Root cause is the REPLACE semantics of set_revocations vs the MAX-merge semantics of note_revocation — the in-memory-first revocation is not durable against a concurrent poll-driven map replacement.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: set_revocations REPLACES the whole map: src/session.rs:168-170 `*self.revocations.write() = rows.into_iter().collect()`. It is called from the poll/reconcile/sync-now path (src/config_db_sync.rs:857-861 inside reopen_and_rebuild_iam, under the ConfigDb mutex). note_revocation (src/session.rs:174-178) records in-memory FIRST and WITHOUT the DB lock (src/api/admin/sessions.rs:85-86, before the lock taken at line 91), so a concurrent poll that wins the DB mutex can set_revocations-REPLACE the map in the window between note_revocation and the revoke's own DB write (or permanently if that DB write fails, line 94-97 setting persisted=false). entry_live reads only self.revocations (src/session.rs:195), so a wiped in-memory epoch = the compromised identity authenticates again. The comment at src/session.rs:166-167 states set_revocations is 'Called on startup, after each revoke, and after a config sync' — i.e. it deliberately overwrites, unaware that a concurrent config sync can overwrite a just-recorded local revocation.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Ranged GET of a legally-stored delta object falls into the buffered TooLarge rejection (same root as the confirmed buffered-GET bug, reached via Range)
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: A delta object whose reference+delta combined size exceeds max_object_size but whose file_size <= spool_threshold (default max_object_size). A client issues a ranged GET (e.g. Range: bytes=0-1023). retrieve_stream_range resolves it as StorageInfo::Delta with file_size <= spool_threshold, so it does NOT take the spooled range path (retrieve.rs:538) and returns Ok(None) at retrieve.rs:557 (small delta/reference → 'use buffered slice path'). The adapter at s3_adapter_s3s.rs:206 then calls engine.retrieve(), which routes through retrieve_buffered → the same combined_size > max_object_size check at retrieve.rs:627-633 → EngineError::TooLarge → client gets EntityTooLarge on every ranged GET, forever, for an object that was written and acknowledged. Same underlying defect as the confirmed buffered-full-GET TooLarge bug, but this is a distinct entrypoint (the range codepath) that also has no size-aware bypass.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: retrieve.rs:554-558 returns Ok(None) for small Delta/Reference ('signal caller to use the buffered slice path'). s3_adapter_s3s.rs:206-209 then calls engine.retrieve() on the None branch, which hits retrieve_buffered's combined_size>max_object_size guard (retrieve.rs:627-633). The range never reaches a path that avoids the buffered TooLarge gate for a small-file_size but large-reference delta object.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Maintenance write-gate is blind to background-job writes (replication + lifecycle) — a copy in-flight when a reencrypt/migrate job arms races the rewrite/flip, corrupting or losing data
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: A replication rule has destination bucket B (or a lifecycle transition writes into B). An operator starts a reencrypt job on B (or a migrate whose SOURCE is B). Replication's control check only calls maintenance_gate.is_busy(&dest_bucket) once per page (worker.rs:1274-1278, via ctrl.check before the copy batch at worker.rs:461); the actual copies (copy_one_object → engine.store on dest_bucket, worker.rs:499) never call gate.write_started/write_finished. So: (1) replication passes the is_busy check for a page (gate not yet armed), (2) the concurrent copy batch begins engine.store on key K in B, (3) the admin arms the reencrypt job (set_busy(B)), (4) the maintenance worker claims it and runs drain_inflight_writes(B) (maintenance/worker.rs:228,492) which reads inflight_writes(B)==0 — because replication never incremented it — and returns IMMEDIATELY, (5) the maintenance worker begins rewriting B via retrieve(K-old)→store(K-old-reencrypted) while replication's engine.store(K-new) is still executing on the same key. Replication's new bytes are silently overwritten with stale re-encrypted bytes (reencrypt case), OR, in the migrate-source case, an object replication/lifecycle writes into the migrate source between the copy and flip phases is never copied to the target and is lost after the flip (permanently deleted if delete_source=true). This is the identical corruption the gate exists to prevent (gate.rs:11-14) and the identical TOCTOU class as the confirmed S3-middleware/admin-bulk bug — but for the background copy paths, which the admin bulk loops (objects.rs run_copy_loop/move/bulk_delete) DO register for via write_started/finished while replication (worker.rs, event_consumer.rs:414, scheduler.rs:147) and lifecycle (worker.rs:853 maintenance_write_bucket_busy) do NOT.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: grep confirms zero write_started/write_finished calls in src/replication/, src/lifecycle/, src/transfer.rs, or src/deltaglider/ — the maintenance drain (maintenance/worker.rs:484-502) waits only on the inflight_writes DashMap counter, which only the S3 gate middleware (gate.rs:171-173) and the admin bulk loops (objects.rs:255/296,410/451,510/547) ever touch. Background copiers gate themselves solely with is_busy at page granularity (replication/worker.rs:1274, lifecycle/worker.rs:853), leaving every in-flight copy invisible to drain_inflight_writes and free to land mid-rewrite. Fix mirror: the background copy units must write_started(dest)/write_finished(dest) around each engine.store (from a Drop guard, per the confirmed leak fix) so the maintenance drain actually waits for them.

confirmed_adjacent.title: S3 CopyObject verb propagates stale dg-encryption markers onto a decrypted body — destination unreadable after encrypted→non-encrypting copy
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Source bucket routes to an encrypting backend (mode aes256-gcm-proxy, key k); destination bucket routes to a non-encrypting backend (mode none / WriteMode::PassThrough). A client issues S3 CopyObject (PUT with x-amz-copy-source) with the default/COPY metadata-directive from src/photo.jpg to dst/photo.jpg. copy_object() calls engine.retrieve() which DECRYPTS the source to plaintext, then at line 935-941 clones source_meta.user_metadata verbatim — which still contains dg-encrypted=aes-256-gcm-v1 and dg-encryption-key-id=k — and passes it to engine.store() (line 948-957) with NO marker stripping. store.rs assigns metadata.user_metadata = user_metadata verbatim (store.rs:614/721/901/979/1060); the dest EncryptingBackend in mode none returns the body unchanged and never touches the markers (encrypting.rs encrypt_if_enabled, 926-945). The destination object is now plaintext bytes carrying encryption markers. Every subsequent GET of dst/photo.jpg fails: is_encrypted(meta)==true → pick_decrypt_key errors 'object was encrypted with key id k, but this backend has NO encryption key configured' (encrypting.rs:996-1005). Unlike the transfer.rs copy paths, this verb has NO strip_user_metadata_keys mechanism at all, and it is directly client-reachable with only copy permission.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:938-941 clones source_meta.user_metadata for the COPY directive and stores it unstripped at 948-957; engine store writes it verbatim (src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:614); non-encrypting dest wrapper never strips (src/storage/encrypting.rs:935-944); read-time hard failure at src/storage/encrypting.rs:996-1005. Compare transfer.rs which at least exposes strip_user_metadata_keys.

confirmed_adjacent.title: Admin bulk-move propagates stale dg-encryption markers AND deletes the source — silent data loss on encrypted→non-encrypting move
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Source bucket on an encrypting backend (key k), destination bucket on a non-encrypting backend (mode none). An operator uses the admin GUI Move action (POST /_/api/admin/objects/move) to relocate objects. run_copy_loop → copy_one (objects.rs:304) does engine.retrieve() (decrypts to plaintext) then engine.store()/store_with_multipart_etag() with meta.user_metadata.clone() (lines 325/337) — stale dg-encrypted / dg-encryption-key-id markers included, never stripped. copy_one performs NO destination verification, so store returning Ok is treated as success. Because copy_result.failed==0, move_objects then DELETES every source object (objects.rs:439). Result: the destination object is plaintext-with-stale-markers and fails on every GET (encrypting.rs:996-1005 'no encryption key configured'), while the only readable copy — the source — has been permanently deleted. Not covered by the confirmed transfer.rs finding: this path bypasses transfer.rs entirely and calls engine.store directly with no strip mechanism.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: src/api/admin/objects.rs:325 and 337 clone meta.user_metadata into store without stripping; copy_one has no verify_destination equivalent; move deletes source after copy_result.failed==0 at objects.rs:407-439; dest markers survive (src/storage/encrypting.rs:935-944) and break reads (src/storage/encrypting.rs:996-1005).

confirmed_adjacent.title: Admin bulk-copy propagates stale dg-encryption markers onto a decrypted body — destination unreadable after encrypted→non-encrypting copy
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: Source bucket on an encrypting backend (key k), destination bucket on a non-encrypting backend (mode none). Operator uses the admin GUI Copy action (POST /_/api/admin/objects/copy). copy_one (objects.rs:304) retrieves+decrypts the source and stores it with meta.user_metadata.clone() (lines 325 for the multipart-etag path, 337 for the plain path) without stripping the dg-encrypted / dg-encryption-key-id markers. The non-encrypting destination stores plaintext-with-stale-markers (encrypting.rs:935-944), and every later GET of the destination object hard-fails with 'object was encrypted with key id k, but this backend has NO encryption key configured' (encrypting.rs:996-1005). Same class as the confirmed transfer.rs bug but in the admin object-copy path, which routes through engine.store directly (not transfer.rs) and has no strip_user_metadata_keys mechanism.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: src/api/admin/objects.rs:325,337 clone meta.user_metadata into store_with_multipart_etag/store with no marker strip; verbatim persist at src/deltaglider/engine/store.rs:614; non-encrypting dest never strips (src/storage/encrypting.rs:935-944); read failure at src/storage/encrypting.rs:996-1005.

confirmed_adjacent.title: form-POST upload path emits object events WITHOUT the is_user_object_key filter, so raw webhooks fire for DG-internal-shaped keys the PUT path suppresses
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: An operator runs event_delivery in format=raw (delivers every claimed event, no should_notify/is_user_object_key re-filter). A browser PostObject upload (form_post.rs) writes an object whose key is chosen by the uploader to be `reference.bin`, `foo/bar.delta`, or `x/.deltaglider/y`. form_post.rs:788 calls enqueue_object_event directly with that key, bypassing the is_user_object_key gate. The s3s PUT path (s3_adapter_s3s.rs:70-79 emit_object_event) DROPS exactly these keys before enqueue. Result: an ObjectCreated outbox row is created for a DG-internal-shaped key via form-POST, and the raw webhook dispatcher (deliver_raw, event_delivery.rs:181-226, which never calls is_user_object_key or should_notify) delivers a spurious notification that an identical upload via the normal S3 PUT path would never generate. The Slack format re-filters via should_notify (slack_format.rs:47) and replication re-filters via match_rules (event_consumer.rs:242), so only the raw-webhook consumer is affected, and the effect is a spurious notification rather than data loss.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: form_post.rs:788 `enqueue_object_event(state, NewEvent::new(EventKind::ObjectCreated, bucket, &parsed.resolved_key, ...))` has no `is_user_object_key(&parsed.resolved_key)` guard, whereas s3_adapter_s3s.rs:77 gates every emit with `if !crate::replication::event_consumer::is_user_object_key(key) { return; }`. The doc-comment on is_user_object_key (event_consumer.rs:160-163) states it is 'Shared by the write-path emit filter and the consumer's routing so DG internals never generate replication work' — form_post is a write path that skips it. deliver_raw (event_delivery.rs:181-226) delivers unconditionally with no filter.

confirmed_adjacent.title: A wedged-but-heartbeating replication consumer pins the webhook prune floor, so webhook-delivered outbox rows grow without bound
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: replication.enabled=true with at least one rule whose destination bucket is permanently maintenance-gated (or the rule perpetually fails). The event consumer drain_once heartbeats the replication listener_cursor every tick (event_consumer.rs:365 advances updated_at even on zero-advance) but never moves last_event_id past the wedged key (failed_ids pins the contiguous watermark, event_consumer.rs:419/457/508). Because the cursor's updated_at keeps refreshing, event_outbox_min_active_listener_cursor (event_outbox.rs:797-813) always treats it as ACTIVE and returns the stuck last_event_id. dispatch_once uses that as min_keep_id (event_delivery.rs:458-463), so ALL three pruners refuse to delete any delivered/failed row above the stuck cursor id (event_outbox.rs:561/591/626 `AND id <= min_keep_id`). Webhooks may have successfully delivered thousands of those rows, but they can never be pruned while the consumer stays wedged-yet-heartbeating → the encrypted config DB grows without bound (the same DB blob is shipped whole on every config-sync upload).
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: event_consumer.rs:365 `dbg.listener_cursor_advance(REPLICATION_LISTENER, row.last_event_id, now)` refreshes updated_at without advancing last_event_id when the watermark is pinned by failed_ids (event_consumer.rs:508 only advances if `watermark > cursor`). event_outbox.rs:806 selects `MIN(last_event_id) ... WHERE updated_at >= cutoff`, so a heartbeating-but-stuck cursor is never treated as stale and always pins the floor. All three pruners gate on `AND id <= min_keep_id` (event_outbox.rs:561, 591, 626). The LISTENER_CURSOR_STALE_SECS mitigation (event_delivery.rs:57, 460) only helps a cursor whose updated_at genuinely stops advancing; the mid-drain heartbeat defeats it.

confirmed_adjacent.title: ListBuckets ignores the client-supplied continuation_token → pagination re-serves page 1 forever, buckets beyond the cap are unreachable
confirmed_adjacent.scenario: A client calls ListBuckets on a proxy that owns more buckets than the effective page cap — either >10000 buckets (default cap, list_buckets_output_from_rows line 2011 `.unwrap_or(10_000)`) or a client that passes `max-buckets=100` with 200 buckets. First response: the adapter sorts all buckets by name, truncates to `cap`, sets `is_truncated=true` and `continuation_token = rows.last().name` (src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:2012-2017). The client, per S3 contract, re-issues ListBuckets with that `continuation-token`. But `list_buckets` (src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:388-409) never reads `input.continuation_token` — it always calls `list_buckets_with_dates()` (which returns ALL buckets, no server-side pagination — confirmed in storage impls, e.g. src/storage/s3.rs:1273, engine/mod.rs:1579-1582), re-sorts, and re-truncates to the SAME first `cap` names. Result: every page is byte-identical to page 1 with the same continuation token → the SDK loops forever (or, for a bounded SDK, silently terminates) and every bucket whose name sorts after the cap boundary is NEVER returned. Data (buckets) is unreachable via the documented pagination API.
confirmed_adjacent.evidence: src/s3_adapter_s3s.rs:392-408: `list_buckets` extracts `input` but only passes `buckets`, `input.prefix`, `input.max_buckets` to `list_buckets_output_from_rows`; `input.continuation_token` is never referenced anywhere in the function or the output builder (grep of the file shows `continuation_token` appears only in the OUTPUT construction at line 2016-2031, never as a read of the input). `list_buckets_output_from_rows` (line 2000-2038) unconditionally starts from the full sorted `rows` and truncates from the front (line 2013-2015), so the offset the token was meant to encode is discarded. No integration test exercises ListBuckets pagination (no `max-buckets`/continuation assertions in tests/), mirroring the untested-guard pattern of the confirmed put_object streaming bug.

critic_gaps.title: S3Lease.renew retry loop uses a single stale `now`, so a genuinely-lapsed lease is renewed and re-extended after transient blips — two nodes can hold the same replication lease
critic_gaps.scenario: Leader node A calls renew() at t=100 (now=100 captured once). Its first read_lease/put_lease attempts hit transient coordination-bucket errors and loop (RENEW_MAX_ATTEMPTS=3), each retry taking >TTL wall time (e.g. B2/coordination backend is degraded for several seconds and the lease TTL is short, or the retries block long). Meanwhile the lease actually lapses (real wall clock passes expires_at) and peer B steals it, writing a NEW lease object. On A's retry, read_lease returns B's fresh lease. plan_renew is evaluated with the STALE now=100 and owner==A: because A passed the owner check on the ORIGINAL object but now sees B's object, owner!=A → Lost (safe here). BUT in the same-owner double-renew window (A re-reads its OWN object whose expires_at it just cannot re-verify against real time — plan_renew checks expires_at >= stale_now=100, which is trivially true for A's own not-yet-overwritten object), A re-extends a lease using now=100 even though real time is past expiry, so expires_at is computed from a stale base. The renewed lease's expires_at = 100 + ttl, which may already be in the past relative to real wall clock, and A believes it still leads. Net: the >= now lapse guard is defeated because now is frozen at loop entry; a slow retry sequence lets A renew a lease it should have lost, enabling a double-run of the same replication rule against one deltaspace (the single-writer-per-deltaspace invariant the lease exists to protect).
critic_gaps.evidence: `now` is a parameter captured once at renew() entry (line 244) and reused for every retry iteration's plan_renew call (line 261) and every expires_at computation (line 248). RENEW_MAX_ATTEMPTS=3 retries (line 253) with no re-sampling of the clock. plan_renew's lapse guard is `lease.expires_at >= now` (line 90) — with a frozen `now`, a lease that lapses in real time DURING the retry sequence still passes the guard. The 412/CAS protection only catches the case where a peer already overwrote the etag; it does NOT catch A re-extending its own still-present-but-logically-expired object.

critic_gaps.title: Session cleanup_expired ignores the revocation epoch, so a revoked-but-not-TTL-expired session lingers in memory and a stale synced revocation map that drops the identity un-revokes it
critic_gaps.scenario: An IAM key AKIA1 is compromised and revoked via note_revocation (in-memory) — entry_live now rejects its sessions. cleanup_expired (line 417) runs on the periodic sweep and retains the session because it only checks `created_at.elapsed() < ttl` (TTL not yet reached), NOT the revocation epoch. The session row stays in the map. Then a config-sync poll calls set_revocations() (session.rs:168) which REPLACES the entire revocations map with the DB snapshot; if the DB write of the revocation raced the poll and the snapshot does not yet carry AKIA1 (or the revocation was only recorded in-memory via note_revocation and never persisted), the revocation entry is gone and the still-resident session validates again. The revoked/compromised key's admin cookie is live again until real TTL expiry.
critic_gaps.evidence: cleanup_expired's retain predicate is `info.created_at.elapsed() < ttl` (line 421) — it never consults self.revocations, unlike entry_live (line 187-200) which is the validity gate. Because the session is never physically removed on revocation, its continued validity depends entirely on the revocations map staying populated. set_revocations (line 168) does a wholesale `*self.revocations.write() = rows.into_iter().collect()` — a full replace that can drop an in-memory-only revocation, and the lingering session is then re-validated. This is the same class as the reported config-db-sync #168 finding but the missing piece is that cleanup_expired does not physically evict revoked sessions, so the in-memory map is load-bearing and its replacement is a live un-revoke.

critic_gaps.title: do_scan totals use unchecked `+=` on u64 for quota accounting — a prefix whose logical bytes sum overflows wraps to a tiny total, silently disabling the quota gate
critic_gaps.scenario: A bucket with a quota policy accumulates delta objects whose LOGICAL file_size sums exceed u64::MAX across a very large prefix (or, more realistically, a corrupt/adversarial FileMetadata.file_size from a degraded xattr read reporting a near-u64::MAX size). do_scan runs `total_size += obj_size` (line 303) with no checked_add. In release builds the add wraps silently; total_size becomes a small number. check_quota (object_helpers.rs:100) then computes `usage.total_size.saturating_add(incoming_bytes) > quota` against the wrapped-small total and ALLOWS a write that should be rejected — the quota freeze is bypassed. Even short of full overflow, a single bogus file_size poisons the cached total for the whole 5-minute TTL, letting unlimited writes through the gate.
critic_gaps.evidence: Line 303 `total_size += obj_size` and line 321 `child.size += obj_size` are unchecked. meta.file_size is trusted verbatim from storage metadata (line 302), which for delta objects is the reconstructed logical size and for degraded xattrs can be arbitrary (CLAUDE.md notes corrupt xattrs are handled gracefully elsewhere but here they feed the accumulator directly). The result is cached (line 248) and consumed by the quota gate (object_helpers.rs:99-107) with only a saturating_add on the incoming side — the accumulated base is already wrong.

critic_gaps.title: decode_aws_chunked accepts a well-framed body when x-amz-decoded-content-length is absent, so a truncated-but-frame-valid streaming PUT can store a short object as complete
critic_gaps.scenario: A client (or a proxy/CDN that re-buffers) sends a STREAMING-UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD-TRAILER PUT but omits or drops the x-amz-decoded-content-length header, and the body is truncated at a chunk boundary such that the last complete chunk is followed by a valid `0\r\n\r\n` terminator that happened to survive (or the truncation lands exactly after a chunk's CRLF and a crafted terminator). get_decoded_content_length returns None (line 87-92), so decode_aws_chunked's length-reconciliation block (line 180-189) is skipped entirely. The decoder returns Some(partial_body) and the proxy stores the truncated content as a complete object. Every subsequent GET serves silently-truncated data with a 200. The module's own doc promises it 'rejects truncated inputs whose decoded length doesn't match what was advertised' — but with no advertised length there is no check.
critic_gaps.evidence: The only integrity reconciliation is gated on `if let Some(expected) = expected_length` (line 180). When the header is missing, expected_length is None (line 87-92 returns Option) and the block is skipped, so a frame-valid-but-content-truncated body is accepted. The chunk-level checks (truncated-chunk at line 158, missing-CRLF at line 173) only catch truncation MID-chunk, not truncation that removes whole trailing chunks while leaving a parseable terminator. AWS SDKs normally send the header, but the proxy sits behind arbitrary clients and intermediaries and cannot assume it.

critic_gaps.title: delete_objects batch path bypasses per-key IAM authorization (independently confirms the reported iam-auth finding)
critic_gaps.scenario: An IAM user with an ABAC policy that Allows delete only on prefix `a/*` but Denies `b/*` sends a DeleteObjects batch containing key `b/secret`. The single-object delete_object handler calls authorize_object_operation (line ~660) before deleting, enforcing the Deny. But delete_objects (line 699) checks only check_client_write_allowed (line 704, the replication_target_only marker gate) and then loops `self.state.engine.load().delete(&input.bucket, &key)` (line 719) with NO per-key IAM authorization call. The Deny on b/* is ignored and b/secret is deleted. Prefix-scoping and Deny carve-outs are silently defeated for any batch delete.
critic_gaps.evidence: The loop at lines 717-719 calls engine.delete directly with no authorize_object_operation / permission check per key, whereas the DeleteObject single path performs authorization (visible at line 660 authorize call before the delete match at 673). The only gate on delete_objects is the client-write-allowed marker check (line 704), which is orthogonal to IAM. This matches the sweep's iam-auth[HIGH] item and I confirm the code path.
